risks.title: Global framework agreements dissolve the 'large operator direct' cell the venture plans to enter first
risks.narrative: The funnel counts 40% of PDU spend as 'large operators' — the venture's declared first buyer. But the number is a capacity-share proxy, not a channel reality. The documented procurement pattern for exactly these buyers is HQ-level global frameworks and integrator bundles: pan-European colos and hyperscalers sign multi-year power-chain packages with Schneider/Vertiv-class suppliers or take PDUs embedded in OEM/ODM rack builds. A regional entrant never sees an RFP for that spend. The cell exists as consumption; it barely exists as a contestable transaction. The independence assumption geography × segment × buyer hides that the biggest buyer cell and the least reachable procurement route are the same cell.

mechanism.text: Large operators consolidate rack-power procurement into global frameworks and integrated OEM/ODM packages: Vertiv's named major customers span Equinix and global telecoms, PDU vendors explicitly market OEM/private-label channels, and ODMs like Digipower supply operators via white-label design programs

The 0.40 cell was derived from 'hyperscale+colo ≈ 62% of capacity and large operators buy direct' — a consumption share, not a channel split; the only attached datapoint (30% of one supplier's orders) measures DC exposure, not who signs the PO. Spend transacted under frameworks negotiated outside CE is recorded in this cell but is foreclosed to a pre-launch regional entrant

cust.operator-large 0.40 overstates the discrete-buyer share of PDU spend; the funnel keeps 40% of TAM inside the venture's first channel while the real contestable route runs through OEM/integrator packages — a different sell with different economics
refs: profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?

risks.whyMissable: The buyer-mix row reads as a demand split, and 'large operators buy direct' matches sales intuition. The ledger flags the node 'needs-source', which reads as a precision problem to fix with interviews — not a reachability problem. The number can be right about who consumes PDUs and still wrong about who awards the contract.
risks.falsifier: Three CE large operators confirming they run discrete, vendor-open rack-PDU RFPs (not framework call-offs or integrator bundles) for their 2026 fit-outs.

likelihood.rationale: Every procurement case surfaced in the corpus (framework agreements, all-in integrated contracts, bundled IT line items) points toward embedded procurement; no counterexample of a discrete open large-operator PDU tender in CE was found. Uncertainty remains on what fraction of the cell is framework-covered.

perturbation.note: Half of the 'large operator direct' cell is framework/bundle-foreclosed to a new regional entrant
Mix-shift: that spend re-appears in the OEM/integrator channel, which requires a different go-to-market

indicators.where: Operator procurement press releases and capital-markets-day supplier disclosures
indicators.threshold: ≥1 additional multi-year framework explicitly including rack PDUs

mitigations.action: Run structured procurement-route interviews with 3 CE large-operator sourcing leads: is rack-PDU spend a discrete RFP line or a framework call-off?

risks.title: Colo-MW geography weights are wrong by construction for the 29% enterprise slice — the Netherlands cell is partly phantom
risks.narrative: Every geo share was apportioned on Statista colocation MW, but the segment axis includes 29% enterprise on-premise demand — and enterprise machine rooms are distributed by industrial base, not by where Amsterdam colo campuses stand. EUDCA's own framing says colo+hyperscale is only about two-thirds of Europe's IT power; the missing third has a different map. NL is the most colo-concentrated market in the set (~70% of its capacity in Amsterdam metro), while Switzerland is self-operation-heavy (installed-base method gives 0.13 vs the curated 0.08) and Germany's installed load exceeds its colo figure by ~1,000MW. Multiplying colo-weighted geography by an enterprise segment share manufactures enterprise PDU demand in the Netherlands that physically sits in German and Swiss machine rooms. Anyone sizing a DACH enterprise channel off these weights will staff the wrong countries.

mechanism.text: Commercial colocation + hyperscale provide only ~two-thirds of Europe's total IT power — roughly a third of real rack-PDU-consuming load (enterprise on-prem) is invisible to the colo-MW denominator the geo shares were built on

The non-colo third is not distributed like the colo two-thirds: NL capacity is colo-hub concentrated, while CH's installed-base cross-check already runs to 0.13 vs the curated 0.08 and DE's installed load exceeds colo by ~1,000MW; the funnel nonetheless multiplies the same colo-derived geo vector against seg.enterprise = 0.29

For ~29% of segment scope the geo weights systematically overstate NL and understate CH (and DE at the installed-load scope) — geo.NL = 0.28 counts an enterprise slice that is not there, so the NL×enterprise cell is partly phantom and the CH×enterprise cell is undersized

risks.whyMissable: Both axes are individually well-sourced — Statista for geography, Synergy for segment — so each ledger row passes review on its own. The error exists only in the product of the two axes; no single node carries it, no band covers it, and the ledger's colo-vs-installed cross-checks read as per-country noise rather than a systematic per-segment misallocation.
risks.falsifier: A consistent country-level installed-IT-load table (e.g., EUDCA country profiles) showing NL's share of CE-7 total installed load ≈ its colo share (~0.28) — i.e., the non-colo third distributes the same way the colo two-thirds does.

likelihood.rationale: The colo-vs-installed divergence is already documented in the ledger's own cross-checks for DE, CH, AT and PL (all diverging in the direction this hypothesis predicts); only the magnitude and the NL side are unconfirmed.

perturbation.note: NL weight corrected for its near-zero enterprise on-prem contribution to the 29% slice
Mix-shift: self-operated Swiss load reallocated in — a reallocation, not a shrink; the risk is misdirected go-to-market, not lost TAM

indicators.where: EUDCA 'State of European Data Centres' country profiles; BMWK/Bitkom German installed-load updates
indicators.threshold: DE/CH installed-load shares diverging >5pp from their colo shares

mitigations.note: Removes the independence assumption where it is demonstrably false

risks.title: The DE×hyperscale anchor cell is a mirage: CE-7's hyperscale-owned share is far below the rescaled global 44%
risks.narrative: seg.hyperscale rescales Synergy's global 48% flat onto Central Europe. But ~60% of Europe's operational hyperscaler self-build capacity sits in Ireland, the Netherlands, Sweden and Belgium — three of the four outside CE-7. Inside CE, hyperscale presence is mostly leased capacity inside Frankfurt and Amsterdam colo campuses, which Statista's 'colocation and scale colocation' figure already counts on the geography axis as colo MW. The cube therefore double-books the same megawatts — hyperscale on the segment axis, colo on the geo axis — and the implied beachhead cell (DE 0.50 × hyperscale 0.44 ≈ 22% of TAM as 'German hyperscale') largely does not exist as hyperscale-owned facilities. The real buyer of that capacity's PDUs is the colo operator or its integrator, with colo-grade requirements: tenant billing metering, multi-tenant DCIM. A product roadmap and sales motion built for hyperscale custom SKUs in Germany aims at a cell the market never populated.

mechanism.text: Synergy's 48% hyperscale share is global; CBRE-sourced reporting places ~60% of Europe's operational hyperscaler self-build in Ireland, Netherlands, Sweden and Belgium — a geography that barely overlaps CE-7 — while the model applies the global split flat with a 0.91 carve-out

Within CE-7, hyperscale demand is predominantly leased: European colocation remains significantly larger than self-build, FLAPD colo hubs (Frankfurt, Amsterdam in CE) absorb the hyperscale take-up, and the Statista geo denominator counts that same 'scale colocation' MW as colo — so the segment axis and geo axis book the same capacity to different segments

seg.hyperscale 0.44 overstates CE hyperscale-owned demand and understates the colo-operator cell; the venture's implied DE×hyperscale×large-operator beachhead is materially smaller, and the real buyers need colo-grade product (billing metering, multi-tenant DCIM), not hyperscale custom builds
refs: profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?

risks.whyMissable: seg.hyperscale is the best-dressed node on the segment axis — 'triangulated' across multiple Synergy vintages with a tight 0.40–0.48 band. The flaw is not the number but its geography: a global capacity split cannot survive contact with a region whose hyperscale capacity is leased rather than owned, and no band on a correctly-triangulated global figure covers a regional composition error.
risks.falsifier: A Europe- or CE-scoped capacity split (Synergy, CBRE or JLL) showing hyperscale-owned/self-build capacity ≥40% of CE-7 total — i.e., the global split does transfer.

likelihood.rationale: The 60%-outside-CE self-build concentration is directly sourced, and Frankfurt's status as a colo (not self-build) hub is well documented; residual uncertainty is how much leased hyperscale capacity should still be attributed to the hyperscale buyer for PDU-spec purposes.

perturbation.note: Hyperscale-owned CE share corrected for the self-build concentration outside CE-7
Mix-shift: leased 'scale colocation' capacity re-attributed to the colo-operator segment where the PDU purchasing decision actually sits

indicators.threshold: CE-7 self-build share of Europe rising above ~25% would weaken the risk; staying ≤20% confirms it

mitigations.note: Aligns the product with the real buyer of the double-booked megawatts

risks.title: TAM is apportioned on capacity stock, but PDU revenue follows fit-out flow — and the flow is concentrating outside CE-7
risks.narrative: The €300M base scopes Europe down to CE-7 using 39.8% of Europe's colocation MW stock. But PDUs are bought at fit-out: revenue tracks new capacity delivered plus refresh, not standing stock. Europe's new-supply flow is heavily skewed — 57% of 2025's 937MW of new colo supply and 85% of take-up land in FLAPD, of which only Frankfurt and Amsterdam are CE-7; the UK alone (1,772MW, Europe's largest market) plus Paris, Dublin and fast-growing secondary markets like Milan and Madrid sit entirely outside the denominator's numerator. If CE-7's share of European fit-out flow is meaningfully below its 39.8% stock share, the €300M base is biased high in a way the 240–360 band — built from global-anchor and FX uncertainty — was never designed to cover.

mechanism.text: European new colo supply is flow-concentrated: 937MW expected in 2025, 57% of it in FLAPD, with 85% of take-up in FLAPD — only two of the five FLAPD markets (Frankfurt, Amsterdam) are inside CE-7

The tamBase chain converts Europe→CE-7 using the 39.8% stock share of colo MW; but the UK (1,772MW, larger than Germany), France, Ireland and seven secondary markets exceeding 100MW are absorbing a disproportionate share of the new-build fit-outs where PDU purchase orders are generated

CE-7's share of European rack-PDU revenue is lower than its share of installed stock; the 39.8% apportionment hop biases the €300M base high independently of the global-anchor uncertainty the sensitivity band encodes

risks.whyMissable: The band 240–360 looks like it already covers 'TAM smaller', so the risk reads as priced-in. But the band was constructed from the global anchor spread and FX, not from the stock-vs-flow choice in the regional apportionment hop — two independent error sources cannot share one band, and the digest's own tensions note the CE-7 denominator is uncorroborated by any independent source.
risks.falsifier: A vendor-share or shipment-based regional table (e.g., the pending Omdia vendor table or IndexBox EU country tables) showing CE-7 at ≈38–40% of European rack-PDU revenue — matching the stock share.

likelihood.rationale: The flow concentration in FLAPD/UK is directly sourced and large (85% of take-up), but part of the flow does land in Frankfurt/Amsterdam, and refresh demand on standing stock partially tracks the stock share — the net bias is real but of uncertain magnitude.

perturbation.note: CE-7 revenue share corrected from stock-weighted 39.8% toward a flow-weighted apportionment

indicators.signal: CE-7 share of quarterly European new colo supply deliveries
indicators.where: CBRE 'Europe Data Centres Figures' quarterly reports (market-level MW delivered)
indicators.threshold: CE-7 (Frankfurt+Amsterdam+secondary CE) sustained below ~35% of European deliveries for 2 consecutive quarters

indicators.where: IndexBox EU Rack PDUs report country tables (the ledger's own pending source); Omdia vendor-share regional cut

mitigations.note: Collapses both the ±40% band and the stock-vs-flow ambiguity in one purchase

mitigations.action: Keep UK/France certification and channel options warm in the launch plan so the venture can follow the fit-out flow if CE-7's share proves flow-diluted
mitigations.note: Cheap optionality against a structurally smaller home region

risks.title: Buyer concentration × procurement timing empties the Year-1 window in the exact cell the ramp assumes
risks.narrative: The 1%-of-SAM Year-1 benchmark implicitly assumes demand arrives smoothly from many independent buyers. But the venture's chosen cell — DE-heavy geography (0.50) times large operators (0.40) — is the lumpiest corner of the cube: a handful of Frankfurt/Amsterdam campus fit-outs whose electrical BoM, PDUs included, is specified at design and tender stage long before delivery. Capacity delivering in the venture's first 12 months was specced in 2024–25; capacity being specced now delivers after the window closes. The correlation between concentration and procurement timing means the truly contestable slice of SAM in months 0–12 is far below what a market-average benchmark implies — the YAM can miss even at the band's low end without a single node being 'wrong'.

mechanism.text: Year-1 demand in the target cell is dominated by a few flagship fit-outs: Frankfurt alone is a 1.2GW market and Europe's 937MW of 2025 new supply is concentrated in a handful of campuses whose electrical procurement is locked at tender, well before the venture's launch window opens

With geo.DE=0.50 and cust.operator-large=0.40, the obtainable pool is not thousands of independent purchase decisions but a short list of lumpy, pre-committed projects — 85% of European take-up flows through FLAPD-scale operators whose specs are set upstream — so the smooth-arrival assumption behind the 1%-of-SAM benchmark fails precisely in this cell

obtainableFactor 0.01 is a market-average Year-1 benchmark applied to a cell whose months-0–12 contestable share approaches zero; YAM is overstated for this specific geography×buyer mix even inside the stated 0.005–0.03 band

risks.whyMissable: obtainableFactor was already caught and corrected once (3%→1%) and now carries 'triangulated' maturity with a conservative-looking band — it reads as the most-scrubbed node in the funnel. The residual error is not the level but the correlation between buyer concentration and procurement timing, which no single-node sensitivity band can encode.
risks.falsifier: Two or more CE large-operator rack-PDU procurements in the venture's first 12 months that are open to unlisted vendors and run from RFQ to award to delivery inside that same window.

likelihood.rationale: Project lumpiness and upstream spec-lock in the target cell are directly documented in the pipeline data; the open question is only whether refresh/retrofit demand inside standing Frankfurt stock provides enough short-cycle volume to rescue the average.

perturbation.note: Year-1 contestable share conditioned on the DE×large-operator cell's spec-lock timing, below the generic band low

risks.title: The €300M base counts the non-intelligent majority of a market the venture cannot sell into
risks.narrative: The venture sells intelligent rack PDUs, but tamBase anchors on Grand View's $2.81B 'rack PDU' figure — and Grand View itself states the NON-intelligent segment held the largest revenue share in 2025. IndexBox confirms the split is real by selling 'Rack PDUs' and 'Intelligent Rack PDUs' as separate EU reports. The scope-down chain carefully audited geography and FX but never audited product type: roughly half the €300M base is basic/metered units the product line does not address. TAM, SAM and YAM all inherit a ~2x product-scope inflation while the ledger label ('rack-PDU market, all segments/buyers') reads as if it were scope-matched.

mechanism.text: Grand View's own segmentation of the $2.81B anchor reports the non-intelligent rack-PDU segment held the largest 2025 revenue share, while the venture's product is intelligent PDUs only

Independent sizings of the intelligent-only subset run $2.1B (GIA 2024) to $3.0B (MRF 2025) against $1.7–3.9B for total rack-PDU — and IndexBox maintains distinct EU reports for 'Rack PDUs' vs 'Intelligent Rack PDUs', confirming the vendor community treats them as different products with different bases

The €300M CE base carries the full-category value; the venture-relevant intelligent-only base is materially smaller, and every downstream funnel stage (SAM €165M, YAM €1.65M) inherits the inflation because serviceableFactor haircuts reach, not product scope

risks.whyMissable: The label 'rack-PDU market' looks perfectly scope-matched to a rack-PDU vendor — the refinement cycle even proved its diligence by removing the PDUs+PSUs ceiling, creating the impression definitional contamination was handled. The remaining contamination sits inside the anchor's own segmentation table, one level below the headline number anyone reads.
risks.falsifier: A scope-pure European source (IndexBox EU Intelligent Rack PDUs headline, or an Omdia segment table) showing intelligent/monitored/switched PDUs at ≥75% of European rack-PDU revenue — i.e., Europe's revenue mix is intelligent-dominated even if global unit mix is not.

likelihood.rationale: The anchor source directly states non-intelligent held the largest revenue share globally; the offset is that intelligent units carry 3–5x ASPs and European colo/hyperscale buyers skew intelligent, so the revenue-weighted European intelligent share plausibly lands at 45–60% rather than below 40%

perturbation.note: Restrict the base to the intelligent-PDU-relevant revenue share of the rack-PDU category (~55% revenue-weighted in Europe)

indicators.signal: Intelligent vs non-intelligent revenue split in the next Grand View / Mordor rack-PDU report vintage
indicators.where: Grand View Research rack-PDU report segment tables; Mordor Intelligence Intelligent PDU report
indicators.threshold: Non-intelligent still ≥45% of European revenue

risks.title: The 'Europe ≈ 30%' hop is an inserted number; two sourced regional shares imply Europe is ≤24%
risks.narrative: The single most load-bearing hop in tamBase — Europe's share of the global rack-PDU market — is stated by no source. Grand View publishes only NA = 38%; MarketIntelo puts Asia-Pacific at 38.2% and calls it the DOMINANT region. If both regional leaders hold ~38%, Europe plus rest-of-world share a ~24% residual, and Europe alone plausibly sits at 20–23%, not 30%. The 30% was inferred from NA's share alone, before the APAC datapoint existed in the corpus. A 7-point regional-share error moves the base ~23% by itself, and unlike the tornado's band it is a directional bias, not symmetric noise.

mechanism.text: No retrieved source states a Europe share of the global rack-PDU market; the chain inserts ≈30% by analogy to Grand View's NA = 38%

MarketIntelo independently reports Asia-Pacific dominated the rack-PDU market with 38.2% revenue share; NA 38% + APAC 38.2% leaves ≤23.8% for Europe, MEA and LatAm combined, capping Europe well below the inserted 30%
tamBase is linear in the Europe-share hop: 30%→23% cuts the CE base ~23%, compounding with (not covered by) the ±20% band, and dragging SAM and YAM down proportionally

risks.whyMissable: The expression reads like sourced arithmetic — '$2.81B × Europe ≈ 30% [est.; NA = 38% per same source]' — so a reviewer sees a citation next to the hop and assumes the 30% is anchored. The bracketed 'est.' is the only tell that the decisive multiplier is invented, and the corroborating APAC datapoint lives in a different publisher's report the model never joined against.
risks.falsifier: A published regional revenue table for the rack-PDU category (Grand View regional chapter, IndexBox world-vs-EU headline pair, or Omdia) showing Europe at ≥28% of global revenue.

likelihood.rationale: Two independent sources sum NA+APAC to ~76%, directly constraining Europe below 30%; the counterweight is that MarketIntelo's category scope may differ from Grand View's, so the two shares may not be strictly additive

perturbation.note: Europe share corrected from 30% to ~23% residual; all other hops held constant

indicators.signal: Europe regional revenue share in any rack-PDU-specific report vintage
indicators.where: Grand View rack-PDU report regional segmentation; IndexBox world benchmark report vs EU report headline ratio
indicators.threshold: Europe stated or derivable at <27%

risks.title: Colo-only apportionment plants the flag in Germany/NL while 35–40% of PDU demand sits in non-colo load with a different country mix
risks.narrative: Every geo share divides colo MW by colo MW, but rack-PDU demand tracks TOTAL installed IT load — and the non-colo remainder is not distributed like colocation. Germany's installed load (BMWK >2,700MW) exceeds its colo figure by ~1,000MW, Switzerland is self-operation-heavy (installed-basis share 0.13 vs 0.08), and Poland's better-corroborated installed-load figure yields 0.10 vs 0.07. EUDCA confirms the gap is continental: colo+hyperscale is only about two-thirds of Europe's IT power. A venture that builds its Year-1 named-account plan on the colo-derived map will staff Frankfurt and Amsterdam while a third of the actual PDU spend — enterprise and self-operated halls in CH, PL, AT — never appears on its target list.

mechanism.text: EUDCA: commercial colo+hyperscale provides just over two-thirds of Europe's IT power — so roughly a third of PDU-consuming load is outside the colo scope every geo.* ratio is built on

The colo-vs-installed divergence is country-specific, not uniform: DE's installed-load method gives 0.42 vs colo 0.50, CH's self-operated stock pushes it to ~0.13 vs 0.08, and PL's triangulated 660MW installed load implies ~0.10 vs 0.07 — the €300M gets reallocated across countries, not just rescaled

The Year-1 plan sells through large operators (colo-centric by construction), so the misallocated map inflates the reachable share of DE/NL and hides demand in CH/PL enterprise halls; the funnel's geography mix shifts against the venture's channel build
refs: profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?

risks.whyMissable: Each geo node already discloses its colo-vs-installed crossCheck divergence, so the issue looks acknowledged — but it is acknowledged as per-country BAND WIDTH, not as a correlated, same-direction reallocation across all countries at once. Bands on independent nodes cannot express 'the whole dimension is measured in the wrong denominator'.
risks.falsifier: A single-scope, single-source table of total installed IT load for all CE-7 countries (e.g., EUDCA country profiles) whose per-country shares match the current colo-based shares within ±3 percentage points.

likelihood.rationale: The scope gap is documented in the ledger's own crossChecks for four of seven countries and corroborated continentally by EUDCA; the uncertainty is whether non-colo PDU spend per MW is comparable to colo (enterprise refresh cycles are slower, partially offsetting the reallocation)

perturbation.note: Installed-load method share; mix shift, deliberately not renormalized
NL colo-heavy market loses relative weight under installed-load scope
Self-operated Swiss stock enters the denominator
Mordor/R&M-corroborated 660MW installed load

risks.title: tamBase apportions revenue by standing MW, but PDU revenue follows new-build flow — which is concentrating outside CE-7
risks.narrative: The CE-7 = 39.8%-of-Europe hop divides standing colocation stock by standing stock. But rack-PDU revenue is fit-out-driven: it is earned when MW are delivered or refreshed, not while they sit installed. CBRE shows the flow concentrating away from CE: 57% of 2025's 937MW of new European supply lands in FLAPD (of which only Frankfurt and Amsterdam are CE-7), 85% of take-up is FLAPD, London alone exceeds 1GW and is Europe's largest market, and double-digit growth in secondary markets is going to Milan and Madrid — not Warsaw or Vienna. If CE-7's share of European capacity ADDITIONS is meaningfully below its 39.8% share of stock, the €300M systematically overstates where 2026 PDU purchase orders will actually be written, even if every input number is individually correct.

mechanism.text: European colo revenue-generating activity is flow-shaped: 937MW of new supply in 2025, 57% of it in FLAPD, and 85% of take-up in FLAPD — a distribution measurably different from the standing-stock table the CE-7 ratio is computed from

London (>1GW, Europe's largest market) and Frankfurt together represent roughly half of European supply, and CBRE's tracked secondary-growth markets are Milan and Madrid — so the growth increment that a 9% CAGR is made of accrues disproportionately to non-CE-7 geographies, while CE-7's standing stock skews toward older, lower-density halls with slower PDU refresh

The 39.8% stock ratio overstates CE-7's share of near-term PDU revenue; tamBase should be read as a stock-weighted ceiling, with the flow-weighted (order-relevant) base materially lower — a bias no band edge in the tornado captures because it is a method error, not parameter noise

risks.whyMissable: Stock apportionment is the standard top-down move and every input is sourced and recent — nothing on the page looks estimated. The rot is in an unstated physical assumption (PDU € per standing MW is uniform across regions and vintages) that no source states because no source measures it; you only see it by asking what event actually generates a PDU invoice.
risks.falsifier: CBRE's 2025–26 delivery pipeline (or Statista's 2026-vintage country deltas) showing CE-7 capturing ≥38% of new European colocation MW delivered — i.e., flow share matching stock share.

likelihood.rationale: The FLAPD concentration of additions and take-up is well documented, but Frankfurt and Amsterdam are themselves FLAPD members inside CE-7, which claws back part of the flow; the net CE-7 flow deficit vs its stock share is real but plausibly 5–10 points rather than catastrophic

perturbation.note: Re-weight the CE-7 share from standing stock toward capacity-additions flow (~32% vs 39.8%)

risks.title: The €360M ceiling was set with the same contaminated-category trick it claimed to remove — the real base may sit above the band and the plan is sized for the wrong market
risks.narrative: Refinement cycle 1 lowered the band top from €495M to €360M by comparing the BIS ceiling's 21% growth against a '10–12% intelligent-PDU CAGR (IndexBox)'. But that 10–12% figure comes from IndexBox's €8.5–9.5B EU 'Power and Cable Management' report — a category several times broader than rack PDUs — so the correction that purged one contaminated number imported another. Meanwhile Germany alone is forecast at $509.6M in a PDU category by 2031, already exceeding the entire CE-7 base, and the global anchors span $1.7–3.94B, a 2.3x spread wider than the band. If the true CE base sits at €400M+, the failure mode is not embarrassment — it is a Year-1 plan whose certification scope, channel headcount, and manufacturing commitments were sized for €165M of SAM while incumbents scale into the €80–120M the model never showed the board.

mechanism.text: The band-top-lowering comparator ('10–12% intelligent-PDU CAGR, IndexBox') is sourced from the EU Power and Cable Management report — a €8.5–9.5B multi-category basket — not a rack-PDU report, replicating the exact definitional bundling the refinement flagged in the BIS ceiling

Independent evidence points above the band: Germany's broader-PDU forecast alone reaches $509.6M by 2031, and same-category 2025 global anchors span $1.7B to $3.94B — a spread that exceeds the ±20% band and makes the €360M cap an artifact of one contested comparison rather than a bound

With tamBase capped at €360M, SAM and the Year-1 obtainable plan are calibrated low; under-sizing systematically under-provisions certification breadth (serviceable reach) and channel capacity in the launch window, which is unrecoverable in a market where gear is specified 12–24+ months ahead

risks.whyMissable: Downward refinements read as rigor — 'we caught an inflated ceiling and cut it' is exactly what a diligent team wants on the record, so nobody audits the comparator that justified the cut. Upside error also feels safe ('conservative is fine'), hiding that under-sizing misallocates real resources: too-narrow cert scope and too-thin channel for the market that actually exists.
risks.falsifier: The IndexBox EU Rack PDUs report headline (once purchased) landing at or below €360M for the 2025/2026 EU base, confirming the lowered ceiling despite the contaminated comparator.

likelihood.rationale: Multiple broader-category datapoints point above the band, but each carries the same definitional contamination in the upward direction; the honest read is that the ceiling is unproven rather than proven wrong

perturbation.note: Base above the current band top, consistent with the Germany-alone $509.6M signal and the upper global anchors

risks.title: The 'refuted' GPU-pod doubt was only half-refuted: dense enterprise AI refresh ships on DC busbar, hollowing seg.enterprise from both ends
risks.narrative: Refinement cycle 1 closed the 'GPU pods skip PDUs' doubt because NVIDIA reference architectures ship PDU variants. But the vendor publishes parallel architectures: the AC/PDU version is the fallback, while the flagship dense design routes power via DC busbar with materially lower PDU attach per rack. The only part of enterprise on-premise that is growing is exactly this GPU-pod build-out; the legacy base is bleeding ~2pp of capacity share per year toward 19% by 2031. So the 0.29 enterprise slice is squeezed twice — the shrinking legacy half keeps buying PDUs it already owns, and the growing AI half is steered toward the substitute. A checkbox marked 'confirmed' is carrying 29% of the TAM.

mechanism.text: Enterprise on-premise capacity growth is now driven almost entirely by GenAI/GPU infrastructure after a sustained no-growth period, and the flagship reference architecture for the densest pods uses DC busbar with sharply reduced rack-PDU attach, with the AC/PDU variant positioned as legacy fallback

The growing sub-segment of enterprise carries falling PDU content per rack while the legacy on-prem base shrinks ~2pp/year (from 32% end-2025 toward 19% by 2031) — effective enterprise rack-PDU demand falls on both the growth side and the base side simultaneously

seg.enterprise 0.29 (crossCheck marked 'value confirmed') overstates the PDU-addressable enterprise slice; during the venture's 12-month window the realistic value sits below the model's own 0.24 low band, and the funnel's second-largest segment quietly deflates

risks.whyMissable: The doubt was explicitly investigated and stamped 'refuted' in refinement cycle 1 — the risk register shows a closed item. The binary confirmed/refuted framing hides that PDU-shipping and PDU-attach-per-MW are different questions: NVIDIA shipping PDU variants does not mean the dense builds that constitute all the segment's growth actually use them.
risks.falsifier: Attach-rate evidence from 2026 CE enterprise GPU-pod deployments (integrator BOMs or vendor order data) showing rack-PDU counts per rack at or above the legacy ≥3-per-rack norm in the majority of dense installs.

likelihood.rationale: The parallel-architecture steering is documented in vendor reference designs and the enterprise share decline (2pp/year) is a multi-vintage Synergy trend; uncertainty is whether CE enterprises actually deploy the flagship density tier within 12 months or stay on air-cooled AC/PDU designs

perturbation.note: GPU-driven growth adopts busbar attach while legacy base decays; effective PDU-addressable enterprise share drops below the model's 0.24 low band

risks.title: Wholesale colocation inherits hyperscaler power specs: the 18% colo slice — modeled with zero sensitivity band — follows the substitute out of category
risks.narrative: The model treats colocation as the PDU-native safe harbor: seg.colocation carries a null sensitivity band, so it never appears in the tornado at all. But CBRE shows European colo take-up being outstripped by hyperscaler and AI/HPC tenant demand — and in wholesale/prelet halls the tenant, not the operator, dictates the rack power architecture. If hyperscale tenants specify busway plus power shelves inside leased halls (the same migration the exemplar tracks in self-build), the colo segment's PDU content leaves the category through the tenant spec sheet while the operator's capacity numbers keep growing 19% a year. The one segment the engine treats as certain is the one with the untracked contagion path.

mechanism.text: European colocation take-up is expected to outstrip new supply driven specifically by hyperscaler and AI/HPC tenants — the demand filling CE colo halls is the same buyer cohort migrating to busway/power-shelf architectures, and in wholesale preleases that tenant writes the fit-out power spec

As hyperscale tenants standardize substitute architectures inside leased halls, colo PDU content per MW falls even as colo capacity grows near double-digit and supply across the top-5 markets grows a record 20% — capacity metrics stay healthy while category-specific demand erodes

seg.colocation 0.18 is single-source with a null band: the engine registers zero downside for this slice, so a tenant-spec-driven erosion of colo PDU demand is structurally invisible in every sensitivity output the client's team has reviewed

risks.whyMissable: Colocation reads as the segment where rack PDUs are structurally mandatory (per-rack metering, multi-tenant billing), and the node's null band means it never surfaces in any sensitivity view — the team's eyes are drawn to banded nodes. The kill path runs through the wholesale/retail split inside the 18%, a distinction the model does not carry at all.
risks.falsifier: Fit-out specifications from 2026 CE wholesale colocation preleases (Frankfurt, Amsterdam) showing rack PDUs — tenant-supplied or operator-supplied — as the standard rack power distribution in AI-density halls rather than busway/shelf architectures.

likelihood.rationale: Tenant spec authority in wholesale preleases is well documented and the buyer cohort is verifiably the migrating one, but retail colo and standard-density wholesale (the majority of current CE colo MW) remain PDU-native; the risk concentrates in the AI-density prelet growth tranche within the 12-month window

perturbation.note: Wholesale/prelet AI-density share of colo (roughly a third of the slice) migrates to tenant-specified busway/shelf architectures

risks.title: The 0.91 rescale imports hyperscale that isn't in CE-7: the segment mix is misallocated even though the TAM headline doesn't move
risks.narrative: seg.hyperscale applies Synergy's global 48% hyperscale capacity share to Central Europe via a flat 0.91 carve-out. But ~60% of Europe's operational hyperscale self-build capacity sits in Ireland, the Netherlands, Sweden and Belgium — three of the four outside CE-7 — and Europe's self-build growth (24% YoY) keeps landing disproportionately in those markets. CE-7 is structurally colo- and enterprise-weighted relative to the global mix the model borrowed. Because segment shares sum to the same base, the TAM headline is untouched and no alarm rings; but the SAM haircut logic, certification priorities and Year-1 pipeline are all built on a buyer mix that overweights hyperscale by perhaps a third. This is a resourcing kill, not a sizing kill.

mechanism.text: Synergy's global 48% hyperscale share is transplanted to CE-7 through a flat 0.91 carve-out, but Europe's hyperscale self-build capacity is concentrated (~60%) in Ireland, NL, Sweden and Belgium — a geographic pattern that makes the global split a poor proxy for CE-7

European hyperscale self-build grows 24% YoY versus colo's 19%, and that growth keeps concentrating outside CE-7, so the divergence between the borrowed global mix and the true CE-7 mix widens through exactly the venture's entry window rather than converging

The €300M base is misallocated across segments: hyperscale overweighted, colo/enterprise underweighted. Since shares sum to the same TAM, the headline never flags the error — but which buyers to certify for, which channels to build and where Year-1 pipeline effort goes are all keyed to the wrong weights

risks.whyMissable: A wrong segment mix leaves TAM, SAM and YAM totals identical, so every number the client's team has reviewed reads the same; the tornado tests each share's band in isolation but never tests whether the mix as a whole was transplanted from the wrong geography. The 0.91 carve-out looks like a careful adjustment, which disguises that the underlying split was never Europe-scoped.
risks.falsifier: A Europe- or CE-scoped capacity split (CBRE, Synergy regional cut, or JLL) showing hyperscale-owned/operated capacity at ≥40% of CE-7 MW, validating the transplanted global share.

likelihood.rationale: The 60%-outside-CE concentration figure is directly sourced and the ledger's own promotion path admits no Europe-scoped split exists; the main uncertainty is how much hyperscale leased-in-colo capacity within CE offsets the self-build concentration elsewhere

risks.title: The venture sells intelligent PDUs; the TAM counts all rack PDUs — the category boundary mismatch is inside the model, not at its edge
risks.narrative: Every boundary discussion has focused on excluded substitutes (busway, shelves). The unexamined boundary is in the other direction: the product is intelligent rack PDUs, but tamBase is explicitly scoped as the 'Central Europe rack-PDU market, all segments/buyers' — including basic and metered-only units the venture does not sell. IndexBox itself maintains a separate EU 'Intelligent Rack PDUs' report distinct from its 'Rack PDUs' report, i.e., the industry's own taxonomy treats these as different products. No retrieved source states the intelligent share of European rack-PDU revenue, and the 10–12% intelligent growth figure the model cites traces to a far broader power-and-cable-management report. The ±40% band prices measurement error on the whole category; it says nothing about what fraction of that category the venture can even quote into. Both errors stack.

mechanism.text: The product is intelligent rack PDUs while tamBase covers all rack PDUs; IndexBox sells 'Intelligent Rack PDUs' and 'Rack PDUs' as separate EU reports, confirming the vendor taxonomy splits the subset the venture sells from the category the model sized

The intelligent subset's cited 10–12% growth comes from the €8.5–9.5B EU Power-and-Cable-Management report — a category far broader than rack PDUs — and the directly-scoped rack-panel figure grows only 5–8%; no source quantifies the intelligent share of EU rack-PDU revenue, so the subset fraction is unknown and plausibly well below 1.0

The €300M base overstates the venture's category-true TAM by whatever revenue share basic/metered-only PDUs hold in CE; the sensitivity band (240–360) models measurement error of the full category, orthogonal to and stackable with the subset mismatch — and the one report that would resolve it sits behind a $4,000 paywall

risks.whyMissable: 'Rack PDU' reads as one category and intelligent PDUs are its premium tier, so value share is assumed to approximate category revenue; the wide ±40% band creates the comfortable impression that any downside is already priced in. The band and the subset mismatch are independent errors, and the founder's risk list treats the band as covering both.
risks.falsifier: IndexBox EU Intelligent Rack PDUs headline (or the Omdia rack-PDU tracker's segmentation) showing intelligent/managed units at ≥80% of EU rack-PDU revenue, confirming the subset ≈ category in value terms.

likelihood.rationale: Data-center buyers skew heavily toward metered/switched units and intelligent SKUs carry a large price premium, which pulls value share up — but basic PDUs still dominate unit volumes in enterprise and retail-colo racks, and no source pins the revenue split; genuine coin-flip territory on whether the mismatch exceeds ~25% of the base

perturbation.note: Intelligent (monitored/switched) PDUs assumed to hold ~65% of CE rack-PDU revenue; the remainder is basic/metered volume outside the venture's product boundary

risks.title: The best-triangulated geography is the trap: NL's 28% sits on a capped colo base while its growth migrates to self-build architectures a regional entrant can't quote into
risks.narrative: geo.NL is the model's most reassuring node — Statista and the Dutch Data Center Association agree within 3%. But that triangulation validates yesterday's colocation stock, not the composition of forward demand. The Netherlands is one of four countries holding ~60% of Europe's hyperscale self-build capacity, and self-build is growing 24% YoY versus colo's 19% — while Amsterdam (≈70% of NL colo) is supply-constrained. So the venture's second-largest geography weight (28% of the base) rests on a slice where the PDU-native part is capped and the growing part procures through hyperscaler global frameworks and busway/shelf architectures that a pre-launch regional entrant cannot reach in its first 12 months. The MW totals will keep rising; the biddable NL demand won't.

mechanism.text: NL is among the four countries concentrating ~60% of Europe's operational hyperscale self-build capacity, and European self-build additions grow 24% YoY — in NL, forward capacity growth increasingly happens outside the 951MW colo base that geo.NL's 0.28 apportionment measures

Self-build capacity procures power distribution through hyperscaler global specs rather than regional channels, while the colo segment — larger but slower at 19% YoY and slowly losing share of total capacity — is the only NL demand a new CE entrant can actually bid; Amsterdam supply constraints cap even that

geo.NL 0.28 overstates the entrant-reachable Dutch slice during the pre-launch first-12-months window: the ratio is anchored to a stalling colo stock while the growth it implicitly promises accrues to out-of-category, out-of-channel self-build

risks.whyMissable: geo.NL carries the ledger's cleanest triangulation (two independent sources within 3%), so it reads as the one geography nobody needs to re-examine. Agreement between sources measuring the same colo stock says nothing about whether that stock is the demand a Year-1 entrant will face — the precision of the snapshot hides the composition shift.
risks.falsifier: 2026 NL capacity-addition evidence (DDCA annual report plus fit-out tenders) showing new Dutch MW — including hyperscale campuses — specifying rack PDUs procured through channels open to regional vendors at rates proportional to NL's 28% weight.

likelihood.rationale: The self-build concentration and growth differential are directly sourced and Amsterdam's constraints are well documented; the offset is that NL retail/wholesale colo refresh demand is real and PDU-native, so the question is degree — whether reachable NL demand supports 0.28 or something closer to 0.20–0.22 of the base

risks.title: 83% pre-let pipeline plus long-lead electrical frameworks leave almost no contestable Year-1 PDU orders
risks.narrative: The obtainableFactor's own cross-check assumes DC electrical gear is locked 12–24 months before fit-out, which keeps a ≤2% Year-1 share plausible. But the FLAP-D development pipeline is 83% pre-let at a record-low 6.3% vacancy — the 2026–27 builds a new entrant would sell into had their electrical bill-of-materials specified inside framework agreements signed at pre-let, i.e. in 2023–24. The venture's first 12 months open onto a market where the visible demand is real but already awarded: what remains contestable is churn and expansion inside operating halls, a fraction of the 1% anchor. The YAM is not conservative; it is counting orders that were signed before the company existed in the market.

mechanism.text: FLAP-D vacancy hits a record-low 6.3% with the pipeline 83% pre-let — capacity is committed to named tenants years before commissioning, and pre-let deals carry integrated electrical packages fixed at design lock

PDU line items ride those pre-let frameworks through the large-operator channel the venture targets first; RFPs that open during the venture's first 12 months are for capacity commissioning in 2028+, not for Year-1 revenue

The pre-let statistic lives in commercial real-estate reports (JLL EMEA year-end), not in any PDU market report, so nobody on the team joins the two documents; the funnel has no 'already-awarded vs contestable' dimension where this could surface.

risks.title: A single metro's 24–36-month grid queue gates ~37% of the CE TAM's Year-1 flow
risks.narrative: Germany is 0.50 of the base and Frankfurt hosts ~75% of German IT capacity — so roughly 37% of the venture's TAM routes through one metro's grid interconnection queue, where waits now run 24–36 months and vacancy is 2.2%. The model treats DE as a diversified half of the market; in the first-12-months window it is really a bet that Frankfurt's 542 MW under construction energizes on schedule. If 2026 energizations slip right — grid queue, plus EnEfG efficiency/heat-reuse compliance adding commissioning friction — the fit-outs that would generate the venture's largest tranche of Year-1 PDU purchase orders move to 2027–28 while the 9% trend stays intact on paper.

mechanism.text: Frankfurt grid connections face 24–36-month waits while 542 MW sits under construction and 383 MW in planning — energization slots, not demand, set commissioning dates

Frankfurt is ~75% of German capacity and DE is 0.50 of the CE base, so a queue slip in one metro defers the largest single block of near-term fit-out demand; PDU POs land at fit-out, so deferred energization means deferred orders regardless of the 2030 CAGR
DE's effective share of the in-window demand flow falls toward the installed-load low bound (0.42) and the Year-1 obtainable pace drops with it — the funnel's biggest geography becomes 2028 revenue

risks.whyMissable: The ledger carries country-level shares that look diversified across seven markets; there is no metro dimension, so the fact that half the funnel physically routes through one substation queue is structurally invisible. The DE band (0.42–0.53) reads as scope uncertainty, not as a timing gate.
risks.falsifier: TenneT/regional grid operator commitments (or JLL Frankfurt prints) showing Frankfurt live IT load stepping up by >100 MW within the venture's Year-1, i.e. the 542 MW under construction energizing on schedule.

likelihood.rationale: Queue lengths and near-zero vacancy are documented; offsetting, projects already under construction typically hold secured grid allocations, so slippage hits the planning cohort harder than the UC cohort.

perturbation.note: DE share of contestable in-window demand falls to the installed-load low bound as Frankfurt additions slip
Year-1 pace compressed as the largest geography's fit-outs defer

risks.title: The Netherlands' 0.28 slice is legally and electrically frozen for the entire Year-1 window
risks.narrative: geo.NL = 0.28 is one of the best-triangulated nodes in the ledger — Statista and the DDA agree within 3%. But both measure installed stock, and the venture sells into flow: new fit-outs where a new vendor can be designed in. In NL that flow is administratively shut for the whole first-12-months window: a national decree bans new hyperscale builds outside two municipalities, Amsterdam's draft zoning plan caps connections at 350 MVA until 2030, and distribution firms say no power is available until 2030 — with Amsterdam holding ~70% of national colo. What remains in NL is replacement demand inside operating halls, the most incumbent-locked business there is. Nearly a third of the funnel is a stock share masquerading as an addressable flow.

mechanism.text: National hyperscale ban outside Het Hogeland/Hollands Kroon, Amsterdam's 350 MVA connection ceiling to 2030, and grid operators reporting no power available until 2030

Amsterdam Metro holds ~70% of Dutch colo capacity, so the caps bind the bulk of the country; NL growth already decelerated to 4.9% CAGR 2021–23 — the flow of new fit-outs where a new PDU vendor gets specified approaches zero in the Year-1 window
NL's share of contestable Year-1 demand is far below its 0.28 installed-capacity share; the geography mix overweights a frozen market and inflates SAM and YAM proportionally

risks.whyMissable: The node is triangulated with two independent sources agreeing within ~3% — it looks like a solved cell. The failure mode is not measurement error but metric choice: installed MW is the wrong proxy for a new entrant's reachable demand in a market where new construction is banned and the grid is allocated to 2030.
risks.falsifier: Liander/Stedin announcing material new data-center grid allocations before 2030, or the DDA reporting >80 MW of newly commissioned Dutch colocation capacity within the venture's Year-1.

likelihood.rationale: The ban, the MVA cap and the grid unavailability statements are enacted or on the record for exactly this window; leakage exists via the Eemshaven/Agriport exception zones and private-substation workarounds like the Pure DC 78 MW campus, so the freeze is partial rather than total.

perturbation.note: Contestable NL slice shrinks in absolute terms (frozen new-build flow); deliberately not re-normalizing siblings — this is a smaller slice, not a mix shift

indicators.signal: Adoption status of Amsterdam's umbrella zoning plan and the 350 MVA connection ceiling
indicators.where: Gemeente Amsterdam planning docket and Dutch Data Center Association policy updates
indicators.threshold: Plan adopted with the 350 MVA cap intact

indicators.where: DDA 'State of the Dutch Data Centers' annual report
indicators.threshold: National colo MW flat at ≤950 MW

mitigations.action: Obtain Liander/Stedin/TenneT NL connection-queue data for the data-center category to quantify how much NL fit-out actually proceeds through 2027
mitigations.note: Replaces the stock-based 0.28 with a flow-based reachable share

mitigations.action: Concentrate NL go-to-market on the exception zones (Eemshaven, Agriport) and private-substation projects where new builds are still legal and powered

risks.title: Year-1 hyperscale demand lands in Ireland, Sweden and Belgium — the 0.44 CE slice is a global split painted on the wrong map
risks.narrative: seg.hyperscale rescales Synergy's global 44–48% capacity share onto Central Europe via a flat 0.91 carve-out. But ~60% of Europe's operational hyperscale self-build capacity sits in Ireland, the Netherlands, Sweden and Belgium — three of the four outside CE-7 entirely, and the fourth banned from new hyperscale construction. The 2026 self-build wave (4.2 GW, +24% YoY) will follow existing land banks and secured grid positions, i.e. mostly outside the venture's geographic scope, while inside CE-7 hyperscalers consume capacity as colo pre-lets procured through global frameworks. The model's largest segment is substantially a demand flow the venture cannot geographically reach in Year 1, and the 'sell to large operators first' strategy is aimed at it.

mechanism.text: European hyperscale self-build additions of 4.2 GW in 2026 (+24% YoY) concentrate where the operational base already sits: ~60% in Ireland, NL, Sweden, Belgium as of Q4 2025

Within CE-7 the two plausible hyperscale destinations are gated — NL closed to new hyperscale builds outside two municipalities, Frankfurt queued 24–36 months — so CE's realized hyperscale share of the Year-1 demand flow runs well below the flat 0.91 rescale of the global split

seg.hyperscale at 0.44 overstates the in-scope segment; the overstatement propagates into the large-operator buyer cell the venture leads with, compounding the channel-mix uncertainty already flagged there
refs: profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?

risks.whyMissable: The node is marked triangulated across multiple Synergy vintages, and the trajectory (41→48%) is genuinely well-evidenced — but every vintage is global. The flat 0.91 carve-out looks like a conservative haircut, so the review instinct is satisfied; nobody asks whether the geography of hyperscale capacity is itself skewed away from CE-7, because the ledger has no node where that question lives.
risks.falsifier: A Europe-scoped capacity split (CBRE, Synergy custom cut, or DC Byte) showing CE-7 hyperscale-operated share at or above ~0.40, or two-plus announced 2026 CE-7 hyperscale self-build campuses breaking ground outside the NL exception zones.

likelihood.rationale: The 60% concentration outside CE-7 is a hard Q4-2025 datapoint and the CE gating mechanisms are enacted; uncertainty remains because hyperscalers also occupy CE colo capacity (leased, not self-build), which partially preserves the segment's PDU demand inside scope.

perturbation.note: CE-realized hyperscale slice shrinks in absolute terms; not a mix shift to siblings — the demand flows to geographies outside scope

indicators.signal: CE-7 share of new European hyperscale self-build MW
indicators.where: CBRE European data centre quarterly updates; DCNN/DC Byte self-build geography breakdowns
indicators.threshold: CE-7 receiving <15% of new self-build MW over two quarters

indicators.threshold: Zero new CE-7 self-build campus starts over two consecutive quarters

risks.title: Construction has plateaued while the pipeline balloons: Year-1 opens onto a digestion trough the 9% trend averages away
risks.narrative: EMEA capacity under construction has plateaued at ~2.5 GW even as the development pipeline swelled 25% to nearly 15 GW — projects are stacking in planning, not converting to builds, because grid, permits and capex phasing bind. PDU purchase orders follow construction fit-out, not pipeline announcements. And with public cloud providers taking 61% of 2025 EMEA take-up, three or four hyperscaler capex committees synchronize the whole cycle: if 2026 is an AI-capex digestion year, CE fit-out starts pause for 2–4 quarters exactly inside the venture's window while the 2030 CAGR remains intact on paper. The 1%-of-SAM Year-1 anchor implicitly assumes an on-trend year; the conversion data says the window may be a below-trend one.

mechanism.text: EMEA under-construction capacity plateaued at ~2.5 GW while the pipeline grew >25% to ~15 GW — the planning-to-construction conversion, not demand, is the binding constraint

Public cloud accounts for ~61% of EMEA take-up, so a synchronized capex digestion by a handful of buyers stalls CE fit-out starts for multiple quarters; PDU orders are placed at fit-out, so Year-1 order flow decouples from the 8–10% trend consensus that the model treats as context
obtainableFactor's 1%-of-SAM anchor presumes an average demand year; a trough year roughly halves the achievable Year-1 pace without changing TAM or SAM at all

risks.whyMissable: The CAGR node is triangulated at 8–10% across three publishers and looks solid — but annual lumpiness never appears in a CAGR. The under-construction plateau statistic sits in a real-estate H2 update, not in any PDU market report, and the model has no node representing 'which year of the cycle the launch window lands in'.
risks.falsifier: Cushman & Wakefield / JLL H1-2026 EMEA prints showing under-construction capacity breaking materially above ~2.5 GW with CE construction starts rising, or hyperscaler earnings guidance re-accelerating European data-center capex for 2026.

likelihood.rationale: The plateau and buyer concentration are documented, but 'digestion year lands exactly in the launch window' is a timing call; CBRE simultaneously projects record 20% supply growth in Europe's five largest markets, so evidence points both ways.

perturbation.note: Trough-year order flow: achievable Year-1 pace at half the on-trend anchor

indicators.where: Microsoft/Alphabet/Amazon/Meta quarterly earnings calls, EU infrastructure line items
indicators.threshold: Two or more guiding EU DC capex flat-to-down for 2026

Keep manufacturing and inventory commitments flexible (contract manufacturing, low fixed minimums) to survive a two-quarter order trough without cash strain

risks.title: The declared 'large operators' beachhead is pre-signed: global framework agreements close the 40% buyer cell before the first sales call
risks.narrative: The venture's own profile names large operators as the first buyer, and the ledger prices that cell at 0.40 of PDU spend with a comfortable 0.33–0.47 band. But the band models uncertainty about SIZE, not AVAILABILITY. The procurement record shows large operators buying rack power through multi-year global framework agreements and all-in integrated contracts (the Digital Realty–Schneider $373M framework pattern; Iren's all-in Dell deal; Jülich bundling PDU into a single IT line item). A framework agreement is not a competitor to out-sell — it is a contract that removes the tender from the market for its whole term. If a majority of the 0.40 cell is framework-governed, the entrant's declared beachhead is contractually closed for exactly its Year-1 window, and the YAM is silently re-based onto the slower mid-market and channel cells.

mechanism.text: CE large operators renew or extend multi-year global rack-power framework agreements with Schneider/Vertiv/Eaton — the same vendors whose named major customers (Equinix, Telefonica, Vodafone) are the CE operator base

PDU line items ride the framework or the integrated OEM package; no standalone, contestable RFQ ever reaches the market for that operator's builds during the term — the entrant's pre-launch, first-12-months window (profile: Stage) coincides with mid-term, not renewal, for most frameworks

The addressable fraction of cust.operator-large collapses from 0.40 to roughly the framework-gap residual (~0.20); because the ledger's four channel values are unsourced internal estimates, nothing in the model currently resists this collapse

risks.whyMissable: 'Large operators buy direct' reads as good news on the founder's risk list — direct means no channel margin and a nameable account list. The surface appearance of reachability (you know exactly who to call) hides the contract structure: direct purchasing at that scale is framework purchasing, and the sensitivity band on the node models estimation error, not contractual foreclosure.
risks.falsifier: Two or more CE large operators (Equinix/NTT/Data4-class) issuing standalone, multi-vendor rack-PDU RFQs open to non-framework vendors within the next 12 months.

likelihood.rationale: Multiple documented procurement artifacts (Digital Realty–Schneider framework, Iren all-in contract, Jülich bundled line item) all point the same direction; what is uncertain is the fraction of CE operator spend covered, not the mechanism's existence.

perturbation.note: Framework-governed spend removed from the addressable large-operator cell; residual is framework gaps, expiries and carve-outs
Year-1 pace slows further because the remaining open demand sits in channels the venture did not plan to enter first

risks.title: Annual line reviews plus Legrand's dual-brand grip close the distributor cell mid-cycle — the entrant arrives with the shelf already allocated
risks.narrative: The 0.13 distributor cell looks like the easy channel: no framework agreements, documented master-distributor models, even explicit private-label options for distributors in buyer's guides. But distribution in this category runs on annual line reviews with exclusivity and margin-program terms, and the intelligent-PDU shelf specifically is consolidated — Legrand owns BOTH Raritan and Server Technology, the two intelligent-PDU specialist brands, giving one vendor two slots on every line card and strong leverage in review negotiations. A pre-launch entrant hits the market mid-cycle: even a willing distributor cannot onboard a conflicting line until the next review, which lands at or after the end of the venture's Year-1 window. The cell is not small — for the YAM horizon it is closed.

mechanism.text: Master-distributor structures (the MaxPower pattern: one distributor supplying all reseller partners) concentrate line-card decisions into few annual review events, and Legrand's ownership of both Raritan and Server Technology means the intelligent-PDU slots on those line cards are already double-occupied by one incumbent

The venture launches mid-review-cycle (profile: pre-launch, first 12 months); the documented private-label route exists but requires certifications, stock commitments and margin programs a Year-1 entrant cannot yet fund, so no CE distributor can list the product inside the YAM window

cust.distributor (0.13, maturity needs-source, zero corroborating evidence in the ledger) contributes nothing to Year-1 reachable demand; the funnel silently overstates the channel mix available inside the obtainable window

risks.whyMissable: Distribution looks like the LOW-commitment channel — 'anyone can get listed eventually' — and the ledger's 0.13 looks too small to interrogate. The hidden variable is timing: line reviews are annual gates, so a cell that is genuinely open in steady state is closed for precisely the 12-month window the YAM measures. Founders model channel share, not channel calendar.
risks.falsifier: A named CE electrical/IT distributor (Rexel, Sonepar, Also-class) confirming in writing that a new intelligent-PDU line can be onboarded within two quarters without waiting for the annual line review or triggering an exclusivity conflict.

likelihood.rationale: Annual line reviews with incumbency advantages are the documented norm in electrical distribution (base-rate), and the Legrand dual-brand consolidation of the intelligent-PDU shelf is a hard fact; the open question is how rigidly CE distributors enforce review timing for a differentiated product.

perturbation.note: Distributor cell unavailable to the venture within the Year-1 horizon — foreclosed by review-cycle timing and shelf exclusivity, not shrunk

indicators.where: Rexel Germany / Sonepar online catalogs; Also and Ingram Micro DC-infrastructure category pages
indicators.threshold: Raritan + Server Technology + APC occupying all intelligent-PDU listings with no non-incumbent brand added over two quarters

mitigations.action: Time distributor outreach to the review calendar and pre-book slots for Year-2 rather than burning Year-1 sales capacity on a closed gate

risks.title: Near-zero-margin PDU bundling inside power+cooling packages means the contestable Year-1 pool is below the model's own 0.5% floor
risks.narrative: The obtainableFactor band (0.5–3%) assumes there exists a pool of standalone rack-PDU purchase decisions a new entrant can compete for. But the same incumbents sell the $23B power and $26B cooling packages those PDUs plug into, and the procurement record shows PDUs travelling as embedded line items inside those packages — where the incumbent can price the PDU at near-zero margin because the busway, UPS and cooling carry the deal. The only standalone PDU procurements surfaced by research were tiny ($58K–$130K), had 1–2 bidders, and one was explicitly sole-sourced. Meanwhile the 1%-of-SAM anchor itself is generic startup folklore: the sources define SOM as a 1–3 or 3–5 year cumulative window, and the closest worked hardware comparable runs at ~0.1% of SAM. Both legs of the 1% — the benchmark and the tender evidence — point below the band, not inside it.

mechanism.text: Incumbents holding both the power ($23B) and cooling ($26B) packages bundle intelligent PDUs into integrated deals, cross-subsidising PDU price to zero incremental margin — no bake-off is ever held on the PDU line

The residual standalone-tender pool is small and often non-contestable (sole-sourced, 1–2 bidders), while the 1% Year-1 benchmark the node anchors on is defined by its own sources as a multi-year cumulative figure — Prospeo frames SOM as 1–3 years, Wise as 3–5, and TryBuildCo's worked example lands at ~0.1% of SAM

True Year-1 obtainable share of SAM falls to ~0.3% — below the band's 0.5% floor — because the denominator (SAM) includes bundled demand that never becomes a contestable decision, and the Tractian cross-check that supported ≤1% is itself unverified against its source ($200K starting revenue, not $1.2M first-year)

risks.whyMissable: The founder's team already 'corrected' this node once (3%→1%) and the band looks conservative, so it reads as the model's most stress-tested number. The hidden failure is compositional: the band debates WHICH percentage of SAM is winnable, while the bundling mechanism removes demand from the contestable denominator entirely — a smaller pie of decisions, not a smaller slice of spend.
risks.falsifier: A logged pipeline of ≥5 standalone, multi-vendor rack-PDU tenders in CE-7, each ≥€250K, posted within any 6-month window, in which a non-incumbent vendor is qualified to bid.

likelihood.rationale: The bundling evidence (integrated contracts, sole-sourced standalone bids) and the benchmark-window semantics both push the same direction, but retrofit and colo tenant purchases do generate some genuinely standalone PDU decisions that the tender search may have under-sampled.

perturbation.note: Contestable-decision pool shrinks the effective Year-1 capture below the modeled 0.5% floor

indicators.signal: Ratio of bundled vs standalone rack-PDU procurement in published CE tenders
indicators.where: TED (tenders.europa.eu) — compare PDU-specific CPV lots vs PDU lines inside integrated electrical-fit-out lots
indicators.threshold: ≥80% of PDU volume appearing only inside integrated lots over two quarters

indicators.where: Vertiv and Schneider quarterly earnings calls; Schneider capital markets day materials
indicators.threshold: Explicit statements that rack PDU is sold predominantly as part of integrated rack/power/cooling solutions

mitigations.note: Directly converts the node from folklore benchmark to observed decision volume

mitigations.action: Target the purchase moments bundles don't reach: colo TENANT-side PDU purchases and brownfield retrofit/metering upgrades, where no incumbent package exists to hide the PDU inside
mitigations.note: Retrofit demand is decision-rich even when new-build demand is bundled

mitigations.action: Price and package for attach to third-party rack integrators' bundles rather than head-to-head against incumbent bundles

risks.title: The OEM/integrator cell is squeezed shut from both sides: brand-spec deals go to incumbent ecosystems, white-label seats go to scale ODMs
risks.narrative: The 0.22 OEM/integrator cell looks like the natural entry channel for an industrial entrant — and superficially the evidence agrees, because BOTH routes into it are documented: integrators buy branded PDUs, and buyer's guides advertise OEM/private-label options. The trap is that each route is closed for a different reason. Every integrator case study found shows selection of branded incumbents on certification, DCIM compatibility and support ecosystems — assets a Year-1 entrant does not have. The white-label seats, meanwhile, are held by Asian ODMs (the Digipower pattern) competing on a manufacturing cost curve a Central European industrial entrant cannot match. The cell's 0.22 value is an unsourced internal estimate with an addressability premise no source supports; for this venture the genuinely open fraction may be a third of the modeled cell.

mechanism.text: Rack integrators select PDU brands on features, certification and DCIM-ecosystem compatibility — the documented integrator behavior favors incumbents whose monitoring stacks are already deployed — while the private-label demand that does exist is served by dedicated OEM/ODM manufacturers with global scale

An industrial entrant sits in the dead zone between the two: no installed DCIM/support ecosystem to win brand-spec designs (those accrue to the Legrand/Raritan/Server Technology axis and the top-5 vendor set) and no ODM cost position to win price-spec white-label seats

Addressable share of cust.oem falls from 0.22 to ~0.08 (the fraction of integrator demand that is regional, spec-flexible, and not price-anchored to ODM quotes); the funnel's channel mix shifts further onto cells that are themselves contested

risks.whyMissable: The cell appears doubly open — 'we can sell branded OR white-label' — which is exactly why it survives founder scrutiny. Two documented routes in feels like redundancy; it is actually two separately-owned gates, and the evidence that each gate is held (integrator brand loyalty; ODM incumbency) sits in different source types that never appear on the same page of a risk review.
risks.falsifier: One CE rack integrator or OEM signing a design-in or white-label agreement with the entrant, or a documented integrator RFQ in which a non-incumbent, non-ODM intelligent PDU is evaluated on merit within the launch year.

likelihood.rationale: Every retrieved integrator case study shows incumbent-brand selection and no source quantifies an open OEM share, but the sample of case studies is small and integrator behavior for differentiated intelligent-PDU features is untested — judgment on how far the pattern generalizes.

perturbation.note: Only the regional, spec-flexible integrator fraction remains addressable; brand-spec and ODM-price fractions foreclosed

indicators.signal: PDU brands named in European integrator/turnkey rack case studies and reference designs
indicators.where: Vertiv, Legrand, Rittal and Schleifenbauer project case-study pages; DataCenterDynamics build coverage
indicators.threshold: ≥3 consecutive quarters of new CE integrator projects specifying only incumbent-brand PDUs

indicators.signal: ODM intelligent-PDU pricing and OEM-program activity
indicators.where: Digipower and comparable ODM price lists / OEM program pages; Computex and Data Centre World exhibitor announcements
indicators.threshold: ODM per-outlet-managed PDU quotes ≥30% below the entrant's achievable unit cost

mitigations.note: Replaces the unsourced 0.22 addressability premise with observed gate criteria

mitigations.action: Differentiate on the one axis neither incumbents nor ODMs own regionally: MID-compliant billing-grade metering and local engineering support for CE colo sub-metering requirements
mitigations.note: A regulatory-technical feature can reopen brand-spec gates that price and ecosystem cannot

mitigations.action: Offer integrators a co-branded (not white-label) program with shared support obligations, splitting the difference between the two closed routes

risks.title: In the intelligent-PDU sub-market the venture actually plays in, Legrand's dual acquisition plus DCIM spec-lock makes concentration — and the serviceable haircut — worse than the headline
risks.narrative: shape.cr3 = 0.55 is computed for the whole rack-PDU category, and serviceableFactor's 0.55 haircut is justified purely on regulatory reach (EN 50600, ecodesign, CE/TÜV). Neither number describes the venture's actual product market: INTELLIGENT PDUs, where Legrand owns both specialist brands (Raritan and Server Technology), Vertiv holds Geist, and Schneider leads the category — so the intelligent sub-segment is materially more concentrated than the blended 0.55 suggests. Concentration in intelligent PDUs is not just competitive pressure; it is a serviceability constraint, because intelligent-PDU purchases are spec-locked to DCIM/monitoring ecosystems and vendor-certified compatibility lists. Demand written into specs as 'Raritan-compatible' or 'certified for our DCIM' is not reachable at any price. The 0.55 serviceableFactor, built only on regulatory logic, silently assumes all regulatory-clear demand is spec-open — the one assumption the sub-market's ownership structure contradicts.

mechanism.text: Legrand consolidates the intelligent-PDU specialist brands (Raritan AND Server Technology) while Vertiv holds Geist — the intelligent sub-segment's effective concentration exceeds the blended rack-PDU CR3 of 0.55, which is itself extrapolated from a single 15.8% datapoint

Intelligent-PDU procurement is written against DCIM/monitoring ecosystems: buyer's guides treat DCIM compatibility as a primary selection criterion and the intelligent-PDU sub-market carries only qualitative 'Medium' concentration labels with no share table (Omdia's vendor-share data is paywalled) — so the spec-locked fraction of demand is real but unmeasured

serviceableFactor's 0.55, derived solely from the regulatory stack, omits an ecosystem-compatibility haircut; applying it drops effective serviceable share toward 0.40 — the venture's SAM overstates reachable demand even where every certificate is in hand

risks.whyMissable: The team has a serviceable haircut AND a concentration figure, so both boxes look ticked. What hides the risk is scope mismatch: the CR3 describes a broader category than the product, the serviceableFactor describes a narrower constraint (regulation) than reality, and no ledger node connects them — the DCIM spec-lock falls exactly into the seam between two nodes that each look individually handled.
risks.falsifier: The Omdia rack-PDU vendor-share table showing intelligent-PDU top-3 combined share below ~50%, or ≥3 CE operator/colo PDU specifications requiring only open protocols (SNMP/Redfish/Modbus) with no vendor-certified DCIM compatibility list.

likelihood.rationale: The ownership consolidation is documented fact and DCIM compatibility is a documented selection criterion, but the actual spec-locked fraction of CE demand is unmeasured — the perturbation size rests on judgment over hard shares, which the paywalled Omdia table could resolve either way.

perturbation.note: Regulatory-clear but DCIM/spec-locked demand removed from the serviceable share

indicators.where: TED tender technical annexes; colo operator technical-standards documents (Equinix/NTT design guides); Data Centre World CE conference specs sessions

risks.title: Cyber Resilience Act (Reg. EU 2024/2847): the conformity clock for a networked PDU, started today, ends after the Year-1 window closes
risks.narrative: An intelligent PDU is a 'product with digital elements' under the CRA. Vulnerability-handling and 24h ENISA incident-reporting obligations apply from 11 September 2026 and full conformity (CE marking against CRA essential requirements, SBOM, coordinated vulnerability disclosure, support-period declaration) from 11 December 2027 — squarely overlapping a pre-launch venture's first 12 months. The harmonized standards CEN/CENELEC must deliver are not yet published, and remote-management functionality risks classifying the product as an 'important product' requiring third-party assessment through a notified-body queue that does not yet exist at scale. Worse, data-center buyers front-run: because PDUs installed in 2026 live in racks past 2027, procurement specs are already demanding CRA-readiness attestations today. The serviceableFactor's 0.55 haircut was built on the old CE/TÜV/ecodesign stack; it prices none of this, and the obtainableFactor's 1% Year-1 pace assumes the product can be quoted into 2026 tenders at all.

mechanism.text: CRA obligations land inside the entry window: reporting duties from Sept 2026, full conformity Dec 2027, with harmonized standards unpublished and notified-body capacity unbuilt — a fixed regulatory calendar colliding with a pre-launch stage

Large operators and colos, whose gear outlives the 2027 deadline, insert CRA-readiness clauses into 2026 RFQs; the existing cert-stack rationale for serviceableFactor ('justifies <1.0 but doesn't quantify 0.55') already shows the model treats compliance as a static haircut, not a moving gate

Year-1 tenders that are contestable at all are already scarce and pre-locked 12–24+ months ahead; adding a certification artifact the entrant cannot yet produce removes it from the shortlists that make up the 1% anchor
Reach shrinks (CRA-gated demand exits the serviceable pool) and pace slows (Year-1 obtainable share falls below the 1% anchor while incumbents ship CRA-ready roadmaps)

risks.whyMissable: 'CE marking' is already a checked box on the founder's compliance list, and the CRA presents itself as just another CE regulation. The trap is procedural, not substantive: the conformity ROUTE changes (SBOM, vulnerability process, possible third-party assessment) and buyers enforce it via procurement one to two years before the legal deadline — so the risk bites in 2026 sales cycles while the founder's calendar says 'deadline 2027'.
risks.falsifier: Three 2026 CE data-center PDU RFQs (operator or colo) whose specification documents contain no CRA/cyber-conformity or SBOM requirement, plus confirmation that networked PDUs fall in the CRA default (self-assessment) class.

likelihood.rationale: The CRA dates and product scope are enacted law, not forecast; residual uncertainty is only the important-product classification of remote-management PDUs and how aggressively CE buyers front-run the 2027 deadline in 2026 specs.

perturbation.note: CRA-gated (attestation-demanding) demand exits the entrant's reachable pool until conformity artifacts exist
Year-1 pace falls below the 1% anchor as 2026 shortlists require CRA-readiness

indicators.signal: CRA harmonized-standards progress for network/management devices and the important-product classification of remote-access equipment
indicators.where: CEN/CENELEC JTC 13 work programme and the EU Official Journal harmonized-standards citations; Commission CRA implementing-act docket
indicators.threshold: Networked PDUs / remote management listed under Annex III important products, or harmonized standards slipping past mid-2026
