risks.title: MID (2014/32/EU, MI-003) + German MessEG §32: billing-grade PDU metering in German colo is legally foreclosed without a legal-metrology approval the entrant cannot obtain in-window
risks.narrative: Half of this TAM is Germany, and the pitch of an INTELLIGENT PDU is metering. But the moment a colo uses PDU-level kWh data to bill sub-tenants, the meter falls under legal metrology: MID conformity (Annex MI-003) plus German MessEG obligations including §32 registration — a module B+D type-approval path through PTB/notified bodies that routinely takes 12–24 months and constrains the metering hardware design itself. The corpus already surfaced this (Eichrecht metering foreclosure) and noted that serviceableFactor's single qualitative haircut does not distinguish billing-grade from monitoring-grade demand. The kill path: the entrant's flagship feature is exactly the feature that is illegal to use for its highest-value purpose in its largest geography, so German colo tenders that specify 'MID-compliant outlet metering' — increasingly standard as EnEfG pushes operators toward measured billing — are closed to it. The 0.50 DE share silently assumes all German demand is equally reachable.

mechanism.text: German colos shift from estimate/capacity-tier billing toward measured kWh billing (EnEfG cost pressure), and any meter used for sub-tenant billing must be MID-conformant and MessEG-registered — a named legal-metrology instrument, not a product feature

German colo tenders begin specifying MID-class outlet metering; the entrant, lacking type approval, is disqualified at spec stage from the billing-grade slice of the German market — which is the premium, intelligence-led slice it targets — while Germany is 0.50 of the CE base with Frankfurt colo alone at 831 MW

The effective German share addressable to this entrant is materially below 0.50, and the serviceableFactor's undifferentiated 0.55 haircut double-hides it because the ledger itself admits the regulatory haircut has no empirical anchor

risks.whyMissable: ±1% billing-grade accuracy reads as a spec-sheet feature, and the founder's compliance list covers CE/LVD/EMC — product law. Legal metrology is a separate legal regime with its own notified bodies, its own timeline, and no CE-marking overlap; nothing on a competitor's datasheet reveals that its 'MID' badge took a two-year approval cycle and a constrained hardware design. The model's geography split, built on capacity MW, is structurally blind to which MW require which legal class of meter.
risks.falsifier: A survey of German colo billing practice showing measured per-kWh sub-tenant billing remains marginal (capacity-tier/estimate-based dominating) AND no MID-metering requirement in a sample of 2026 German colo PDU specifications — or evidence the entrant's meter module already carries MID type approval.

likelihood.rationale: The legal obligation is documented (MessEG §32 applies to billing meters), but the share of German colo demand that actually specifies MID-grade PDU metering in-window is unquantified — billing practice is heterogeneous per the corpus.

perturbation.note: Billing-grade metered PDU demand in German colo is foreclosed to a non-MID entrant; effective DE share drops toward the installed-load low end

indicators.signal: MID/Eichrecht metering clauses in German colo fit-out and PDU procurement specs
indicators.where: German colo operator RFQs (NTT GDC, maincubes, Telehouse Frankfurt vendor portals); DKE/PTB legal-metrology dockets on data-center sub-metering
indicators.threshold: MID-compliant outlet metering named as mandatory in ≥2 German colo PDU specs

indicators.signal: Incumbents marketing MID-certified PDU meter modules as a line item
indicators.where: Rittal/Bachmann/Schleifenbauer price lists and German product pages (MID variants carry distinct SKUs)
indicators.threshold: MID variants appearing as the default rather than premium option in German channel price lists

mitigations.action: Commission a legal-metrology scoping opinion plus a 10-tender audit of German colo PDU specs to quantify the billing-grade share of DE demand
mitigations.note: Directly resolves how much of the 0.50 DE cell is MID-gated

mitigations.action: Design the meter as a socketed, separately-approvable MID module (or integrate an already-MID-certified third-party metering chip/module) so the PDU inherits an existing approval
mitigations.note: Decouples the 12–24 month metrology clock from the product launch clock

mitigations.action: Position Year-1 in monitoring-grade (non-billing) use cases and non-German markets while the MID path runs

risks.title: RED cybersecurity delegated act (EU 2022/30, applicable since 1 Aug 2025): one WiFi sensor port drags the PDU into EN 18031 conformity and adds two quarters to launch
risks.narrative: If the intelligent PDU ships with any radio — WiFi management fallback, BLE commissioning, wireless environmental sensors — it is radio equipment, and since 1 August 2025 the Radio Equipment Directive's cybersecurity delegated act applies in full. Conformity runs against EN 18031-1/-2, which are harmonized only WITH RESTRICTIONS: where the restrictions bite (e.g., password/access-control configurations), self-declaration is unavailable and a notified body must issue an EU-type examination certificate. For a pre-launch team this is not a 2027 problem like the CRA — it is a today problem that gates the very first shipment. The obtainableFactor's 1% Year-1 anchor already sits on a corrected, fragile benchmark; a 4–6 month notified-body queue on top of an already pre-let, slow-procurement market compresses the sellable window from 12 months to 6–8.

mechanism.text: The launch configuration includes a radio (wireless sensors / BLE commissioning), pulling the product under the RED cybersecurity delegated act — in force since 1 Aug 2025, i.e., before the venture's first shipment

EN 18031's harmonization restrictions force notified-body involvement for common access-control configurations; certificate lead time lands mid-window, and the market's own procurement clock (gear locked 12–24+ months pre-fit-out, few contestable standalone tenders) means a late start forfeits most Year-1 bake-offs
The Year-1 obtainable share, whose 1% value already rests on a benchmark-period correction and a shaky Tractian comparable, is cut roughly in half by a shortened selling window

risks.whyMissable: RED reads as a law about radios, and this is a power-distribution company; the risk hides in an accessory feature (a wireless sensor accessory or BLE setup app) that the electrical engineering team considers trivial. The compliance plan covers LVD/EMC/EN 62368-1 for the PDU itself; nobody owns the delegated act triggered by the cheapest component on the BOM.
risks.falsifier: Confirmation that the launch SKU is wired-only (no radio in any shipping configuration), or an EN 18031 assessment showing the chosen access-control design falls entirely within the harmonized (self-declaration) scope with certificate in hand before first customer ship.

likelihood.rationale: Conditional on product configuration: wireless sensing/commissioning is common in current intelligent-PDU designs, and the EN 18031 restriction traps are documented, but a wired-only launch SKU fully avoids the gate.

perturbation.note: Selling window compressed from 12 to ~6–8 months by notified-body lead time

indicators.signal: EN 18031 harmonization restrictions status (whether the OJ listing's restrictions are lifted or extended)
indicators.where: EU Official Journal harmonized-standards citations under RED; NANDO database queue times for RED cybersecurity notified bodies
indicators.threshold: Notified-body lead quotes >4 months, or restrictions extended into 2026

indicators.signal: Own-product radio scope decision and test-house booking
indicators.where: Internal launch BOM review; test-house (TÜV/DEKRA) quotation and slot calendar for EN 18031
indicators.threshold: Radio in launch SKU with no notified-body slot booked 9 months pre-launch

mitigations.action: Run an immediate RED applicability screen on every launch configuration and get a written EN 18031 gap assessment from a notified body
mitigations.note: Converts an unknown certification clock into a dated one

mitigations.action: De-content the launch SKU to wired-only (Ethernet/serial), shipping wireless sensing as a post-certification accessory
mitigations.note: Removes the gate from the critical path entirely

risks.title: Buyer-side procurement norms (AVL qualification, ISO 27001/IEC 62443 attestations, global frameworks) close the chosen 40% large-operator beachhead for the entire Year-1 window
risks.narrative: The venture's stated beachhead is large operators — modeled as 40% of PDU spend. But that cell is not entered by winning a bake-off; it is entered by surviving an approved-vendor-list qualification: vendor security questionnaires demanding ISO 27001 and secure-development (IEC 62443-4-1) attestations, TÜV/CB-scheme type-test reports, factory audits, and financial-viability checks — a 12–18 month cycle that no law mandates and no market report prices. On top of that, the corpus shows the cell's spend is increasingly channelled through multi-year global frameworks (Digital Realty's $373M Schneider agreement, Iren's all-in Dell contract) and integrator bundles, meaning much of the nominal 0.40 is never tendered at PDU line-item level at all. The 0.40 value is an unsourced internal estimate; the reachable-in-window fraction of it is far smaller. The model books the beachhead as the largest cell; procurement norms make it the slowest.

mechanism.text: Large-operator procurement requires AVL qualification artifacts (ISO 27001 / secure-development attestations, TÜV/CB reports, audits) that a pre-launch entrant does not yet hold — a buyer-side norm, not a legal instrument, so it appears on no regulatory checklist

The spend that does move moves through global frameworks and integrator bundles rather than contestable PDU line items — the corpus documents Digital Realty/Schneider, Iren/Dell and Jülich-style bundling — so even qualified challengers see few discrete PDU decisions in a 12-month window

The contestable large-operator cell is materially below 0.40 and the Year-1 pace within it approaches zero; since this is the declared primary channel, the YAM is effectively being earned from the other 60% of the buyer mix

risks.whyMissable: The follow-up answer 'we sell to large operators first' is recorded as a strategy choice, and the 0.40 cell makes it look like the biggest pond. AVL cycle time and framework lock-up are procurement plumbing — invisible in every market report the model cites (the channel split is admitted to be entirely unsourced), so nothing in the ledger contradicts the comfortable reading.
risks.falsifier: Two CE large operators confirming a fast-track vendor-qualification path (<6 months) for PDU suppliers and at least one 2026 PDU tender from a large CE operator that is open to non-AVL vendors.

likelihood.rationale: Framework/bundled procurement at large operators is directly evidenced in the corpus (named contracts), and 12–18 month AVL cycles are standard for critical-power vendors; uncertainty is whether specific CE colos run lighter qualification for rack-level gear.

perturbation.note: Framework- and bundle-locked spend removed: contestable direct large-operator share for a new entrant
Year-1 pace re-weighted toward slower-to-qualify channels

risks.title: EnEfG §12 + EED Delegated Reg 2024/1364 reporting duties spec-crawl into PDU tenders as certified-accuracy + incumbent-DCIM-integration requirements
risks.narrative: Germany's Energy Efficiency Act obliges data centers to file energy and efficiency data to the Energieeffizienzregister annually (31 March), and the EU's 2024/1364 rating scheme adds a parallel Union-level reporting layer (annual 15 May filings). Neither instrument names PDUs — which is precisely why the model ignores them. But operators discharge these duties through their monitoring chain, so 2026 PDU specifications in Germany and beyond increasingly require certified metering accuracy classes plus validated integration with the incumbent DCIM suites (EcoStruxure, Vertiv Environet, Sunbird) that generate the filings. A new entrant's PDU without a certified accuracy declaration and pre-validated DCIM connectors fails the spec even when its own telemetry is superior — the reporting laws convert the incumbents' software ecosystems into a de facto certification the entrant lacks. The serviceableFactor's 0.55 was set against a product-law cert stack; it does not price an ecosystem-integration gate that binds hardest in the 50% German cell.

mechanism.text: EnEfG and EU 2024/1364 reporting deadlines recur annually through the venture's window, forcing operators to produce auditable energy data — with German colo already publicizing energy metrics (88% RE share) as a compliance posture

Operators push the compliance burden down into procurement specs: certified metering accuracy and named-DCIM integration become mandatory line items in PDU tenders, and the incumbent suites' validation programs (run by the same top-3 vendors) become gatekeepers for challenger hardware

The serviceable share of TAM for an unvalidated entrant sits below the 0.45 band floor in reporting-bound geographies — and the ledger itself concedes the 0.55 has no bottom-up cross-check to catch this

risks.whyMissable: Both instruments bind OPERATORS, not vendors — a founder scanning for laws that name their product finds nothing and files these under 'customer's problem'. The transmission is contractual spec language, not law, so it never appears in a regulatory scan; it appears in lost tenders, one quarter too late.
risks.falsifier: A sample of 2026 German/Dutch colo PDU specifications showing no certified-accuracy or named-DCIM-integration requirements, or evidence that major DCIM suites accept open-protocol (SNMP/Redfish) PDU data for EnEfG/EED filings without vendor-specific validation.

likelihood.rationale: The reporting obligations and deadlines are enacted; the spec-creep transmission into PDU tenders is plausible and consistent with documented integrator behavior (branded-incumbent selection on DCIM compatibility) but not yet directly evidenced at tender level.

perturbation.note: Reporting-bound demand requiring validated DCIM integration exits the entrant's reachable pool

indicators.signal: Accuracy-class and DCIM-integration clauses in CE colo PDU specifications
indicators.where: German colo RFQs and framework specs (Frankfurt operators); Bundesstelle für Energieeffizienz / Energieeffizienzregister guidance updates on required data granularity
indicators.threshold: Rack/outlet-level certified metering named in register guidance or ≥2 tenders

indicators.signal: Openness of incumbent DCIM validation programs to third-party PDUs
indicators.where: Schneider EcoStruxure and Vertiv Environet partner/compatibility lists; Sunbird supported-device database
indicators.threshold: Third-party PDU additions stalling or program fees/queues lengthening

indicators.signal: EU 2024/1364 second-phase requirements
indicators.where: Commission delegated-act docket for the data-centre rating scheme
indicators.threshold: Phase-2 draft mandating sub-facility (rack-level) energy granularity

mitigations.action: Audit 10 recent German/Dutch colo PDU specs for accuracy-class and DCIM clauses to size the gated fraction of the serviceable pool
Prioritize certification into the two dominant DCIM compatibility programs and ship native Redfish/SNMP profiles mapped to EnEfG/EED reporting fields as a product feature
mitigations.note: Makes compliance reporting a selling point instead of a gate

mitigations.action: Co-market with a DCIM-independent monitoring vendor targeting operators who resent incumbent suite lock-in

risks.title: The 1% Year-1 anchor is a 36-month number wearing a 12-month label
risks.narrative: obtainableFactor's 1% rests on Prospeo's staged benchmark, read as an annual snapshot. But Prospeo itself frames SOM as revenue captured over 1-3 years, IdeaPlan says 'one to three years', Wise says 'three to five years', and TryBuildCo's own worked example lands at 0.1% of SAM — below the model's stated floor of 0.5%. The cross-check comparable (Tractian '$1.2M first year') is unverified against its own source, which shows $200K starting revenue. For a pre-launch industrial entrant with zero installed base and no certifications on day 0, the honest 12-month number is the bottom of the reference class, not its midpoint. The whole YAM figure is a period-mismatch error, not a market estimate.

mechanism.text: Every source underlying the 1% benchmark defines the obtainable percentage as a cumulative 1-3 or 3-5 year capture window, not a Year-1 snapshot — the model pins a multi-year ratio to a 12-month YAM

The two cross-checks that were supposed to independently confirm 1% both fail on inspection: TryBuildCo's worked example yields ~0.1% of SAM (below the model's 0.5% floor), and Tractian's revenue started at $200K — the '$1.2M first year' number cited in the derivation does not appear in the source

obtainableFactor 0.01 overstates the honest pre-launch Year-1 capture by roughly 3x; the corrected value sits near 0.3% of SAM, and the sensitivity band's low edge (0.5%) does not even contain the reference-class outcomes

risks.whyMissable: The node is marked 'triangulated' and the refinement cycle already 'caught' one benchmark misread (Year-2 read as Year-1), so the team believes the period question is settled. The surface appearance of a completed correction hides that the corrected source itself defines the percentage over a multi-year window — the fix repeated the same category of error one level down.
risks.falsifier: A documented comparable — an industrial hardware entrant into DC infrastructure — that captured ≥1% of a regional SAM in its first 12 months from commercial launch, with dated revenue evidence.

likelihood.rationale: The window-semantics mismatch is directly visible in the cited sources' own text (Prospeo, IdeaPlan, Wise all describe multi-year windows), and the Tractian cross-check number is unverifiable against its source — this is closer to a documented error than a speculative risk.

perturbation.note: Multi-year benchmark converted to an honest 12-month figure for a zero-installed-base entrant, consistent with TryBuildCo's 0.1% worked example and Tractian's actual $200K start

indicators.signal: Quote-to-PO conversion time in the first CE opportunities
indicators.where: Own CRM pipeline reviewed at month 6; cross-referenced against TED (Tenders Electronic Daily) award timelines for rack-power line items
indicators.threshold: Median cycle >9 months at month 6 implies Year-1 closes are structurally impossible at the 1% level

indicators.signal: Published first-year revenue disclosures of comparable DC-hardware entrants (Asian PDU makers entering EU)
indicators.where: Company registries / Crunchbase / vendor press releases announcing first EU revenue milestones
indicators.threshold: No comparable showing >0.5% regional SAM capture in year 1

mitigations.action: Build a bottom-up pipeline model (named CE accounts × realistic win rate × cycle time × rack capacity) to replace the top-down benchmark before committing the Year-1 plan
mitigations.note: The ledger's own promotionPath names this; doing it pre-launch converts a period-mismatched heuristic into a defensible number

mitigations.action: Re-denominate Year-1 targets in design-wins and framework qualifications rather than revenue, and set the revenue plan on a 36-month SOM curve
mitigations.note: Aligns internal expectations and investor communication with what the reference class actually delivers in 12 months

risks.title: 2026 demand is already specified: the serviceable haircut ignores that reachability has a clock
risks.narrative: serviceableFactor 0.55 is framed as a channel-and-regulatory reach haircut, as if reach were a static property. But for DC electrical gear, the demand that will invoice in the next 12 months was specified at design stage — the model's own cross-check admits 12-24 month procurement lock, and the corpus shows lock-in occurring far earlier (transformer lead times of 48-60 months, hyperscale cycles up to 4-7 years). Meanwhile the entrant's EU compliance stack (WEEE, CE, TÜV, RoHS, plus EN 50600-compatible documentation) consumes 6-9 months from day 0. The intersection of 'demand contestable in months 1-12' and 'entrant qualified to bid' is a fraction of the static 0.55 — the serviceable share within the YAM window is a different, smaller number than the serviceable share of the market in steady state.

mechanism.text: Rack-power specifications for capacity landing in 2026 were locked at design/procurement stage well before the venture's launch date — the model's own cross-check concedes a 12-24 month lock window, and the growth the market shape describes is precisely this pre-committed pipeline

The entrant cannot bid even the contestable residue until its compliance stack clears: the full EU entry set (WEEE, CE, TÜV, Grüner Punkt, RoHS, EORI) is broader than the ledger's named certs and runs 6-9 months, and EN 50600 facility-standard compatibility must be documented for operator RFQs

serviceableFactor 0.55 counts demand that is temporally unreachable within the first 12 months; the effective serviceable share for the YAM window — demand both uncommitted and open to a newly-certified vendor — is closer to 0.35, and the node has no bottom-up cross-check to catch this

risks.whyMissable: The serviceable haircut looks handled because it already discounts 45% of the TAM and cites the regulatory stack. What the static number hides is a timing dimension: 0.55 may be right for the market in general and simultaneously wrong for every month of the venture's first year, because 'reachable' and 'reachable now' are different sets. A team reviewing the number checks its level, not its clock.
risks.falsifier: Three or more CE operator RFQs for rack PDUs in the next two quarters that (a) accept vendors whose certifications complete during the tender process and (b) award within 9 months of publication.

likelihood.rationale: Base rate for enterprise/DC hardware: sales cycles of 12-18 months and certification of 6-9 months are well-established for the reference class; the open question is only what fraction of CE demand is genuinely spot-contestable, which no source quantifies — the absence itself is the risk.

perturbation.note: Static reach haircut tightened to window-adjusted reach: demand that is both uncommitted and open to a vendor whose certification completes mid-year

indicators.signal: Time from RFQ publication to award for rack-power line items in CE
indicators.where: TED (Tenders Electronic Daily) and major CE colo operator procurement portals (e.g., NTT, Vantage, Data4 supplier registration pages)
indicators.threshold: Median award cycle >9 months, or supplier prequalification requiring completed certification at bid time

indicators.signal: Quoted lead time for the venture's own TÜV/CE certification slots
indicators.where: TÜV NORD / TÜV SÜD test-lab booking confirmations and notified-body queue quotes
indicators.threshold: Certification completion date beyond month 8 of the launch year

mitigations.action: Commission a bottom-up channel-coverage model (named CE accounts × spec-lock status × certification-at-bid requirements) to produce a window-adjusted serviceable estimate
mitigations.note: The ledger's promotionPath names this model; adding the spec-lock dimension is the marginal work that prices the clock

mitigations.action: Start the full EU certification stack before launch and pre-register as a supplier on operator procurement portals so month-1 of the commercial window is not month-1 of the compliance clock
mitigations.note: Converts sequential cert→bid timeline into parallel; recovers 4-6 months of the window

risks.title: The chosen beachhead buys through global frameworks: half the large-operator cell is not a market for a regional entrant
risks.narrative: The venture's stated first channel is large operators, and the model gives that cell 40% of PDU spend on the logic 'large operators buy direct'. The corpus shows what 'direct' actually means at that scale: Digital Realty's $373M Schneider framework agreement, Iren's $1.6bn all-in Dell contract, PDU spend embedded as one line inside integrated rack/power/cooling packages. Combined with CR3 ≈ 0.55 among vendors who sell exactly those bundles, the buying event a new entrant needs — a discrete, multi-bidder PDU bake-off at a large operator — is disappearing. The only standalone PDU procurements found in the corpus were small, 1-2 bidder, sometimes explicitly sole-sourced. The beachhead the profile names is the cell most structurally closed to the venture; the model prices it as the biggest open door.

mechanism.text: Large operators consolidate rack-power purchasing into multi-year global framework agreements and integrated vendor packages rather than discrete PDU line items — the exact procurement pattern documented in the cust.operator-large evidence trail

With the #1 vendor alone at 15.8% and a semi-consolidated top set (Schneider, Vertiv, Eaton, ABB, Legrand) selling bundled infrastructure, framework renewal — not open tender — becomes the default buying event; the residual standalone tenders observed were 1-2 bidder or sole-sourced ('No substitutions')

The 0.40 large-operator share — itself an unsourced internal estimate with no channel-split evidence — overstates the spend a regional entrant can actually contest; roughly half the cell is foreclosed behind frameworks the entrant cannot enter in year 1, and the venture's go-to-market points straight at it

risks.whyMissable: The founder's risk list treats 'large operators buy direct' as good news — direct means no distributor margin and a named buyer. The surface appearance of an accessible direct channel hides that 'direct' at hyperscale/large-colo means framework-direct: the door has a lock shaped like a 5-year global agreement, and the buyer's procurement team is contractually discouraged from running the bake-off the entrant's plan requires.
risks.falsifier: Two named CE large operators (e.g., a Frankfurt or Amsterdam colo/hyperscale buyer) confirming a vendor-neutral, multi-bidder rack-PDU RFQ process in 2026 that is open to suppliers outside existing framework agreements.

likelihood.rationale: Framework consolidation at large operators is directly evidenced (Digital Realty-Schneider, Iren-Dell cases in the node's own tension trail), and the customerType split it undermines is admitted to be an unsourced internal estimate; uncertainty is only over the foreclosed fraction, not the mechanism's existence.

perturbation.note: ≈half of large-operator PDU spend sits inside global frameworks and integrated packages that a pre-launch regional entrant cannot contest; siblings deliberately not re-normalized — this is absolute foreclosure, not a mix shift

indicators.threshold: ≥2 new CE-relevant frameworks announced covering rack power

indicators.threshold: Median bidders ≤2 or 'no substitution' language on >half of observed PDU line items

mitigations.note: Second-source slots are the documented entry path for challengers in framework-dominated categories

risks.title: Distributor line-cards and integrator qualification run on annual calendars the 12-month plan cannot intersect
risks.narrative: 35% of the modeled funnel flows through OEM/integrator (0.22) and distributor (0.13) cells — both admitted internal estimates with zero evidence attached. The reference class says both channels have a calendar: distributor line-card reviews are annual events requiring certifications, stock commitments and demand proof in hand; integrators demonstrably select branded incumbents on certification, DCIM compatibility and support (every case study found — Vertiv/Greenergy, Legrand/MIND Park — shows brand selection, none shows a new entrant winning a slot). A pre-launch vendor with no certs on day 0 misses the current listing cycle entirely and enters the next one as an unproven applicant. Within the YAM window these two cells are not small — they are closed, and the model treats 'closed this year' as indistinguishable from 'open at 35%'.

mechanism.text: Distributor line-card onboarding is an annual-cycle gate requiring completed certifications and reference installs at application time — assets a pre-launch entrant does not possess in month 1 and cannot assemble before the cycle closes

The parallel OEM/integrator path is gated the same way but on brand: integrators select PDU suppliers on certification, DCIM compatibility and support track record, a pattern consistent across the corpus's integrator case studies and the incumbent vendor sets that dominate every published landscape

cust.oem (0.22) and cust.distributor (0.13) together carry 35% of the funnel into SAM, but within the first 12 months both cells are calendar-foreclosed; the SAM the Year-1 obtainable percentage is applied to is materially smaller than modeled

risks.whyMissable: Channel shares look like a mix question — 'we'll get some of each' — so the risk review debates the percentages, not their availability. The hidden feature is that indirect channels are not continuously open markets: they are gated queues with annual admission, and a pre-launch company applies to the queue rather than selling into the cell. Nothing in a static share number signals that the cell has an opening date.
risks.falsifier: A signed CE master-distributor listing or OEM/integrator supply agreement with committed volumes, effective within the venture's first six months.

likelihood.rationale: Annual line-card cycles and brand-based integrator selection are standard for the reference class (industrial electrical hardware entering EU distribution); uncertainty is whether an aggressive pre-launch certification push can compress one cycle, which some Asian PDU entrants have managed with EU-stock commitments.

perturbation.note: Distributor cell unavailable within the YAM window — listing cycle cannot complete inside 12 months from a zero-cert start
Integrator cell half-foreclosed: brand/certification selection excludes the entrant from established integrator programs; residual is opportunistic white-label volume

mitigations.note: Stops the plan from booking revenue from cells that have no opening date inside the window

risks.title: The industrial-hardware acceptance base rate un-books a third of whatever Year-1 obtains
risks.narrative: Whatever tiny share the venture obtains in year 1 will be obtained as pilots and first deployments — the profile's own 'conservative ramp' guarantees it. The reference class puts a number on what happens next: 30% of deployed industrial hardware systems are never fully accepted — customers sign, the vendor deploys, and six months later the gear comes back out. Intelligent PDUs are acutely exposed because acceptance hinges on post-deployment behavior: DCIM integration, metering accuracy, firmware stability under the operator's monitoring stack. The model's obtainableFactor is a gross booking rate; nothing in the funnel converts it to net-of-reversal, and for a first-year entrant the reversal doesn't just cut revenue — it destroys the reference accounts that the Year-2 3% benchmark silently assumes exist.

mechanism.text: 30% of deployed industrial hardware systems in the reference class were never fully accepted and required uninstallation — a measured base rate, not an anecdote

A pre-launch entrant's first-12-month wins are structurally pilots/POCs with acceptance clauses; intelligent-PDU acceptance depends on DCIM compatibility and metering behavior that only surfaces after deployment, in mid/enterprise accounts where the venture has no support track record

obtainableFactor 0.01 is a gross capture rate; net of the acceptance-failure base rate, realized Year-1 share runs ~30% lower, and the staged benchmark's Year-2 step (3%) presumes surviving reference accounts that reversals eliminate

risks.whyMissable: Acceptance risk hides inside a revenue number that looks conservative already — at 1% of SAM, no one audits whether the 1% stays booked. The surface appearance of prudence (a small number) conceals that the number is gross, and the funnel has no node where post-deployment reversal could even be expressed.
risks.falsifier: The venture's first three CE pilot deployments passing formal customer acceptance within 90 days of install, with no rollback or return clause exercised in the following two quarters.

likelihood.rationale: The 30% non-acceptance figure is a single-source base rate from adjacent industrial hardware, not PDU-specific; PDUs are simpler than full industrial systems, but DCIM-integration acceptance criteria and zero support track record cut the other way.

perturbation.note: Gross Year-1 capture haircut by the industrial-hardware non-acceptance base rate (~30% of deployments reverse)

indicators.signal: Acceptance and return-rights clauses in pilot contracts
indicators.where: Redlines from CE operator procurement teams on the venture's pilot MSAs, reviewed at each signing
indicators.threshold: Acceptance windows >90 days or unconditional return rights in a majority of pilot contracts

indicators.signal: DCIM interoperability status of the product against the platforms CE operators run
indicators.where: Sunbird/Schneider EcoStruxure/Vertiv Trellis compatibility matrices and the venture's integration test reports
indicators.threshold: Product absent from ≥2 of the major DCIM compatibility lists at launch

mitigations.action: Run a structured pre-launch DCIM interoperability certification program (top-3 platforms used by CE operators) and publish the compatibility matrix before the first pilot ships
mitigations.note: Removes the single largest post-deployment acceptance failure mode before it can trigger the base rate

mitigations.action: Instrument the first five pilots as an acceptance-rate study — formal acceptance criteria, 90-day gates, root-cause logging on any reversal — to replace the generic 30% base rate with a venture-specific one
mitigations.note: Converts the obtainableFactor from a benchmark guess to a measured pipeline parameter, the ledger's own stated promotion path

risks.title: Global framework agreements and bundled fit-outs foreclose the 0.40 large-operator cell the whole GTM is aimed at
risks.narrative: The venture's stated first buyer is large operators, and cust.operator-large carries the biggest single channel weight (0.40) — a needs-source internal estimate with two-decimal precision. The corpus shows what that cell actually looks like from inside procurement: PDU spend at large operators flows through multi-year global framework agreements and integrated rack/power/cooling bundles (Digital Realty–Schneider style frameworks, all-in integrator contracts), not discrete PDU line items a regional entrant can bid. If half the 0.40 is procured this way, the model's most reachable-looking cell is structurally closed in Year 1 — and the conservative 1% obtainableFactor is being applied to a SAM that contains spend no entrant can contest at any price.

mechanism.text: Large operators consolidate power-equipment purchasing into global frameworks and integrator bundles; Vertiv's own partner material lists hyperscalers, telcos and colo REITs as house accounts under standing agreements

The 0.40 share was derived from 'hyperscale+colo ≈ 62% buy direct' — but the only quantitative datapoint behind the channel axis (a supplier's 30%-of-orders figure) measures DC exposure, not buyer-type contestability, and no source quantifies what fraction of 'direct' spend is framework-locked

The contestable portion of the large-operator cell is materially below 0.40; SAM built on the full cell overstates reachable spend for the profile's chosen first buyer, and Year-1 pipeline math against it fails silently
refs: profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?

risks.whyMissable: 'Large operators buy direct' is the founder's own GTM answer, and 0.40 looks like the biggest, most accessible cell. The surface appearance is a channel decision already made; what it hides is that 'direct' at these accounts means procured inside integrated frameworks the entrant cannot bid into — the cell exists, but not for them.
risks.falsifier: Three or more CE large-operator PDU procurements in the next 12 months run as discrete, multi-vendor bake-offs and won by vendors outside the incumbent framework holders.

likelihood.rationale: Every procurement case study retrieved (Jülich, Schneider–Foxconn, Iren–Dell, Digital Realty–Schneider) shows bundled or framework purchasing; no counter-example of a contested standalone PDU line at a large operator surfaced in any search pass. Uncertainty is about the foreclosed fraction, not the mechanism.

perturbation.note: Framework- and bundle-foreclosed portion (~half) of the direct large-operator cell removed from contestable spend; siblings deliberately not re-normalized — this is absolute shrinkage of reachable spend, not a mix-shift

mitigations.note: Aligns the ramp with the cells that are actually contestable pre-brand

mitigations.action: Pursue sub-supplier status inside one integrator's bundle (rack integrator or fit-out contractor) rather than competing against the bundle
mitigations.note: Converts the foreclosure mechanism into a channel

risks.title: A hostile reviewer strikes the unsourced 'Europe ≈ 30%' hop and the €300M loses its floor, not just its band
risks.narrative: The tamBase derivation looks disciplined — a refinement cycle even killed a contaminated €495M ceiling. But its load-bearing hop, 'Europe ≈ 30% of global', is an inserted analyst estimate: Grand View publishes only North America's 38% and no source in any search pass gives a Europe or CE rack-PDU figure. Worse, the global anchor itself is unsettled — same-category estimates run from $1.697B to $5.3B (QY Research $3.255B and Strategic Market Research $5.3B sit in this venture's own digest), a spread wider than the model's ±20% band. A diligence partner strikes the 30%, pairs the lowest credible global anchor with a conservative Europe share, and lands near €200M — below the band's floor — and the model has no sourced fact with which to push back. The band communicates 'we've bounded the uncertainty'; the derivation chain shows the uncertainty was never bounded, only anchored to one publisher's number times an invented ratio.

mechanism.text: Diligence cross-references the global anchor: QY Research puts the global data-center PDU market at $3,255M while Strategic Market Research says $5.3B for rack PDUs alone and other publishers go as low as $1.7B — the primary anchor is one draw from a >2x distribution

With the anchor contested, the unsourced 'Europe ≈ 30%' multiplier and the Statista-based '39.8% of Europe colo MW' CE-7 step (itself scope-inflated vs CBRE's 4,726MW top-15 Europe figure) cannot rescue the chain — every hop is either single-source or inserted

tamBase's effective uncertainty is wider and skewed below the stated 240–360 band; every downstream number inherits a base that cannot survive its first hostile audit, which matters most when the model is used to raise capital or justify CE manufacturing capacity

risks.whyMissable: The refinement-cycle narrative makes the base look stress-tested — a contaminated ceiling was found and removed, which reads as rigor. But the refinement fixed the cross-check while leaving the primary chain's weakest hop (the inserted Europe share) untouched, and even the comparator used to lower the ceiling was itself drawn from a broader-category report. The audit trail creates confidence the audit never earned.
risks.falsifier: The commissioned IndexBox EU rack-PDU country tables (the ledger's own named promotion path) placing the CE-7 base at €270M or above.

likelihood.rationale: The 2.3x global anchor spread and the unsourced Europe hop are documented facts; whether the true value sits below the band floor (vs merely being unverifiable) is a judgment call — the Germany-alone $509.6M broader-PDU figure cuts the other way, so the distribution is wide in both directions but the defensibility failure is certain.

perturbation.note: Low-credible-anchor reconstruction: bottom-quartile global rack-PDU estimate × conservative Europe share × unchanged CE-7 step — the number a hostile reviewer writes in the margin

indicators.signal: IndexBox EU rack-PDU report headline (currently paywalled) or any second Europe-scoped rack-PDU figure
indicators.where: IndexBox price list / report catalog; Omdia and Dell'Oro rack-power publication schedules
indicators.threshold: Any directly-scoped EU figure implying CE-7 <€250M

indicators.where: Grand View, Mordor, QY Research 2026 report refreshes
indicators.threshold: Next-vintage spread across publishers remaining >1.8x

mitigations.action: Commission the IndexBox EU rack-PDU country tables now — the ledger's own promotion path — before the model is shown to any external audience
mitigations.note: Collapses the widest structural uncertainty in the model for the price of one report

mitigations.action: Build a bottom-up cross-check: CE-7 installed MW × racks/MW × PDUs/rack × ASP, so the top-down chain has an independent second method rather than a contaminated comparator
mitigations.note: A 2-method triangulation survives diligence even if each method is imprecise

risks.title: The 0.22 OEM/integrator cell is squeezed from both sides — branded incumbents win the spec, Taiwanese ODMs win the white-label — leaving no seat for a CE entrant
risks.narrative: cust.oem carries 0.22 of the buyer mix with no derivation, no evidence rows, and needs-source maturity — yet it silently props up 22% of the SAM as a fallback channel if large operators prove hard. The corpus says the cell is real but not contestable: every integrator case study found shows integrators selecting branded incumbents on certification, DCIM compatibility and support, while the actual white-label volume is already served by established OEM/ODM specialists (Digipower-class manufacturers) competing on Asian cost structures. A new CE industrial entrant is neither the trusted brand nor the cost-floor ODM. This is not a smaller cell — it is a cell that exists for other players, which is why the correct model operation is exclusion, not shrinkage.

mechanism.text: Rack integrators and distributors source PDUs either as branded components (feature/cert/DCIM-driven) or as private-label from dedicated ODM houses whose entire business is OEM/ODM design at scale

A pre-launch CE entrant fails both selection filters simultaneously: no installed-base/DCIM ecosystem to win the branded path, no volume cost position to win the white-label path — and no source quantifies any historical case of a new regional entrant capturing integrator share

The 0.22 cell should be treated as foreclosed for the Year-1 horizon; SAM and the YAM pipeline built on four contestable channels actually rest on at most three, concentrating execution risk on the already-fragile large-operator cell

risks.whyMissable: The OEM channel reads as a diversification hedge — 'if direct is slow, we white-label.' Qualitative documentation that the channel exists (buyer's guides listing OEM/private-label options) masquerades as evidence that it is open; the founder's risk list checks 'multiple channels' without asking who actually wins each one.
risks.falsifier: Two CE rack integrators or master distributors confirming open white-label PDU sourcing rounds with a realistic qualification path for a new regional manufacturer at viable margins.

likelihood.rationale: Integrator preference for branded incumbents is consistently documented across the retrieved case studies, and the ODM cost squeeze is structural; but the cell is entirely unquantified, and a differentiated intelligent-PDU feature set could crack specific integrators — foreclosure is likely partial rather than total.

perturbation.note: Cell treated as unavailable to this entrant in the modeled window — foreclosure, not shrinkage; distinct from lowering the share

indicators.signal: Brand composition of PDUs in CE integrator rack-build catalogs and reference designs
indicators.where: Rittal/nVent/regional integrator configurators and partner catalogs; Vertiv and Legrand partner-program case studies
indicators.threshold: Zero non-top-5 PDU brands appearing in new integrator reference builds over two quarters

indicators.signal: ODM expansion into Europe (local stock, CE/TÜV-certified white-label lines)
indicators.where: Digipower and peer ODM product/price lists; distributor line-card announcements (e.g., master distributors adding Asian PDU lines)
indicators.threshold: ≥1 major CE distributor adding an ODM white-label PDU line

mitigations.action: Test the cell directly: solicit white-label RFQ terms from 3 CE integrators/distributors to establish real qualification requirements and margin floors
mitigations.note: Converts an evidence-free 0.22 into a priced, dated go/no-go on the channel

mitigations.action: If foreclosed, rebalance the funnel to mid/enterprise direct plus distributor pull-through, and re-cost the Year-1 plan against a 3-channel SAM
mitigations.note: Better to shrink the plan deliberately than discover the phantom channel in Q3

risks.title: NL's 0.28 is a share of a stock that regulation has frozen — growth migrates to self-build hyperscale the entrant reaches through a different (foreclosed) door
risks.narrative: geo.NL looks like the model's safest node: Statista and the Dutch Data Center Association agree within 3%. But both measure the same thing — the existing colo stock — and that stock is capped: Amsterdam's 350 MVA ceiling and grid connections unavailable until 2030 freeze the region holding ~70% of Dutch colo capacity, while NL hyperscale self-build grows at a forecast ~26% CAGR against colo's ~3.5%. The 0.28 therefore describes where the market was, not where the next five years of PDU purchase orders will originate. Worse, the demand that does grow in NL arrives via hyperscale self-build procurement — exactly the framework-locked channel already flagged as least contestable — so the entrant's effective NL opportunity decays on two axes at once: the reachable segment shrinks and the growing segment is behind a closed door.

mechanism.text: Dutch grid and municipal policy (Amsterdam MVA ceiling, hyperscale siting restrictions, power connection queues to 2030) caps new colo capacity in the region that concentrates ~70% of NL colocation

NL demand growth shifts to hyperscale self-build (forecast ~26% CAGR vs colo ~3.5%), a segment measured outside the colo-scoped Statista/DDA figures and procured through global frameworks rather than contestable regional purchasing

geo.NL's 0.28 overstates the entrant's forward-looking NL opportunity: the triangulated agreement between sources validates the frozen stock, not the flow of new PDU demand the Year-1 ramp actually sells into

risks.whyMissable: NL is the model's best-corroborated geography node — two independent sources within 3% earns 'triangulated' maturity and drops off the risk list. The agreement is the camouflage: both sources measure the same capped stock with the same scope, so their concordance says nothing about whether that stock generates purchasable demand over the venture's window.
risks.falsifier: 2026 Statista/DDA updates showing NL colocation MW additions at or above the CE-7 average rate, alongside evidence of Amsterdam-region grid connections being granted before 2030.

likelihood.rationale: The regulatory caps and the colo-vs-self-build growth differential are documented; the uncertainty is timing — legacy-stock refresh cycles (PDU replacement in existing halls) partially offset frozen new-build demand, so the decay is probable but gradual within 12 months.

perturbation.note: Forward-weighted NL share: frozen colo stock discounted for capped additions, with self-build growth excluded as channel-foreclosed; siblings not raised — this is absolute erosion of reachable NL demand, not a mix-shift

indicators.signal: Amsterdam-region grid connection grants and the Amsterdam MVA ceiling's status
indicators.where: Liander/TenneT congestion maps and connection-queue publications; Amsterdam municipality data-center policy docket
indicators.threshold: Ceiling extended or queue lengthening beyond 2030

indicators.signal: Split of NL capacity additions between colocation and hyperscale self-build
indicators.where: Dutch Data Center Association annual 'State of the Dutch Data Centers' report; DC Byte NL market updates
indicators.threshold: Self-build >50% of NL MW additions in the next report vintage

indicators.signal: PDU refresh/retrofit tender activity from existing Amsterdam-area colos
indicators.where: Operator capex commentary (NorthC, Iron Mountain NL, Equinix AM-campus announcements)
indicators.threshold: Visible retrofit programs at ≥3 AMS operators

mitigations.action: Size the NL retrofit/refresh opportunity separately from new-build: survey PDU installed-age profiles across Amsterdam colos to quantify replacement demand the frozen stock still generates
mitigations.note: Replacement demand in a capped market can be attractive precisely because incumbents deprioritize it — but it must be measured, not assumed

mitigations.action: Shift NL positioning to retrofit-optimized SKUs (form-factor compatibility, hot-swap metering) and weight new-build GTM toward PL/CEE where capacity additions are unconstrained
mitigations.note: Matches product-market motion to where the demand flow, not the demand stock, sits

risks.title: The 1% Year-1 anchor is a 36-month accrual read as an annual snapshot — the pre-launch clock makes even that generous
risks.narrative: obtainableFactor's 1% was 'corrected' in refinement cycle 1 and now looks conservative and triangulated. But the benchmark corpus it leans on defines SOM as a 1–3 year (Prospeo, IdeaPlan) or 3–5 year (Wise) cumulative capture, not a Year-1 run-rate, and the one worked example in the corpus lands at 0.1% of SAM — 5x below the band's own floor. Layer the venture's actual clock on top: it is pre-launch, so months 1–6 are certification, listing and pipeline-building, not selling. A 'conservative ramp' that books its first PO in month 7–9 doesn't earn 1% of SAM; it earns a fraction of the back half of the year. The band's low edge (0.5%) is not the pessimistic case — it is the optimistic reading of the sources' own semantics.

mechanism.text: The Prospeo/IdeaPlan/Wise benchmark family defines the 1–5% obtainable share as accruing over 1–3 or 3–5 years, not per-year; the ledger pins 1% strictly to Year 1

The only concrete worked example in the corpus yields SOM/SAM ≈ 0.1% ($3.2M/$3.2B) for years 1–2, and the Tractian comparable started at $200K — not the ~$1.2M first-year figure the ledger's crossCheck asserts
The venture is pre-launch with a conservative-ramp profile: the selling window inside Year 1 is 6–9 months at best, so any annualized benchmark must be pro-rated before it is applied
refs: profile:What obtainable horizon should Year-1 (YAM) assume?

mechanism.text: obtainableFactor at 0.01 overstates the realistic Year-1 capture by 2–3x; the defensible point sits at or below the band floor

risks.whyMissable: The node was already corrected once (3%→1% in refinement cycle 1) and carries maturity 'triangulated' — it reads as the fixed, conservative number. The residual error is not the value but the time-semantics of the sources it cites, which no band edge encodes.
risks.falsifier: A comparable early-stage DC-hardware entrant (intelligent PDU or adjacent rack power gear) documented capturing ≥1% of its serviceable market within its first 12 calendar months from launch.

likelihood.rationale: Three of four benchmark sources in the digest explicitly frame the percentage as multi-year, and the single worked example sits 5x below the band floor; the offsetting uncertainty is that the venture may have pre-launch pipeline the model doesn't credit.

perturbation.note: Pro-rated Year-1 capture consistent with cumulative-window benchmarks and the 0.1% worked example

indicators.signal: Months from launch to first booked CE purchase order
indicators.where: Own CRM plus TED (EU public tender portal) responses to rack-power lots the venture bids
indicators.threshold: No PO by month 6 post-launch

indicators.where: Crunchbase/annual filings of recent PDU or rack-infrastructure startups entering EU
indicators.threshold: Comparables clustering below 0.5% of their stated SAM in year 1

mitigations.action: Build a bottom-up pipeline model (named accounts × win rate × cycle time × months of selling window) to replace the top-down 1% before fundraising commitments are set against YAM
mitigations.note: Directly replaces the contested benchmark with venture-specific arithmetic

mitigations.action: Re-baseline the internal plan on a 24-month obtainable window with quarterly gates, so Year-1 misses don't cascade into a credibility problem with the board

risks.title: The firmware/security audit gate precedes every intelligent-PDU listing — and it isn't in the funnel
risks.narrative: An intelligent PDU is a networked device sitting on the management LAN of critical infrastructure. Before any large CE operator issues a PO, the vendor must clear a security prequalification that a dumb-metal vendor never faces: vulnerability disclosure process, signed-firmware update infrastructure, SBOM, penetration evidence, increasingly IEC 62443-style artifacts as the EU Cyber Resilience Act obligations phase in. This gate is serial with — not parallel to — CE/TÜV product certification, and it is audited against infrastructure the pre-launch venture has to build during the very year YAM assumes it is harvesting. The serviceableFactor's 0.55 haircut names only the classic cert stack; the security gate is a second, slower door in front of the same customers, and it opens per-operator, not per-product.

mechanism.text: Large-operator prequalification for networked power devices requires an auditable firmware update/PSIRT/SBOM capability before AVL listing; the documented EU entry stack (WEEE, CE, TÜV, RoHS, FCC) is already broader than the ledger's derivation names, and cyber requirements sit on top of it

A pre-launch entrant builds this infrastructure and passes per-operator audits inside the same 12 months YAM covers; each audit cycle runs one to two quarters, and the primary-buyer choice (large operators) selects exactly the buyers with the heaviest security gates
refs: profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?

mechanism.text: The gate serially delays the first PO (compressing the effective selling window inside obtainableFactor) and forecloses security-gated accounts entirely for Year 1 (a reach shortfall inside serviceableFactor) — neither effect appears in the static 0.55 or the 1%

risks.whyMissable: The founder's risk list has a 'certifications' line item, and it looks handled — CE/TÜV are known, scheduled, budgeted. The security audit is invisible from the outside because it is buyer-side process, not published standard: it appears for the first time as a 40-page vendor questionnaire attached to the first serious RFQ, after the model's clock has already started.
risks.falsifier: Two or more large CE operators (or their integrators) issuing rack-PDU RFQs in the venture's first two quarters with no firmware-security/SBOM prequalification requirement, or confirming that product CE marking alone suffices for AVL listing.

likelihood.rationale: Security prequalification for networked infrastructure devices is standard practice at hyperscale/large-colo buyers and CRA obligations are phasing in, but mid-market and distributor channels gate far less strictly; probability reflects the venture's explicit large-operator-first choice.

risks.title: Global framework agreements make the 0.40 large-operator cell mostly non-contestable in the venture's window
risks.narrative: The model's biggest customer cell — large operators at 0.40 of PDU spend — encodes the assumption 'large operators buy direct, therefore a direct-selling entrant can compete for them.' But the way large operators buy direct is through multi-year global framework agreements and integrated infrastructure packages with incumbents whose customer lists already span every hyperscaler and major colo. A framework doesn't shrink the cell's spend; it forecloses it: the PDU line is decided once, globally, years at a time, and never comes to regional tender. For a pre-launch regional entrant whose profile explicitly targets large operators first, the nominal 0.40 collapses to the sliver of that spend outside framework coverage — while the model's YAM math quietly credits the whole cell as contestable.

mechanism.text: Incumbent rack-power vendors hold entrenched direct relationships with the exact buyers in this cell — Vertiv's disclosed major-customer list alone covers Equinix and the global telcos/hyperscalers — and data-center demand is concentrating supplier order books around these accounts

The 0.40 value itself is an unsourced internal estimate: no retrieved source substantiates the channel split, and the one hard number cited (30% of one supplier's orders) measures order composition, not buyer-type contestability — so the cell carries no evidence about how much of it ever reaches open competition

If framework-covered spend is removed, the large-operator cell available to a new regional entrant in its first 12–24 months is roughly half its nominal size, and the venture's chosen go-to-market points at the most-foreclosed cell first
refs: profile:Who is the primary buyer you'll sell through first?

risks.whyMissable: 'Large operators buy direct' sounds like an access advantage — no distributor margin, a named-account sales motion the founder knows how to run. The foreclosure hides inside the same fact: direct purchasing at scale means framework purchasing, and framework spend never appears as a losable deal, so the pipeline simply looks mysteriously thin rather than visibly blocked.
risks.falsifier: Three CE large-operator procurement interviews (or won/lost tender records) showing rack-PDU purchases regularly competed line-by-line at regional level outside global frameworks — e.g., annual PDU bake-offs open to non-incumbent vendors.

likelihood.rationale: Framework and integrated-package procurement by large operators is documented behavior in the digest corpus and incumbent customer lists overlap the target accounts almost completely, but the actual framework-coverage share of PDU spend is unquantified — the cell value itself is admitted guesswork in both directions.

perturbation.note: Roughly half of nominal large-operator PDU spend sits under multi-year frameworks/integrated packages and is non-contestable in the venture's window

indicators.threshold: ≥1 new CE-relevant operator signing a global power framework covering rack distribution

risks.title: The growth the CAGR promises was electrically specified before launch day — Year 1 can only sell into the installed base
risks.narrative: Nine percent CAGR reads as a tailwind: new halls, new racks, new PDU line items every quarter of Year 1. But new-build DC electrical procurement is locked at design freeze, and with long-lead power equipment (transformers, switchgear) now booked years out, operators are locking entire electrical BOMs — PDUs riding along — far earlier than the 12–24-month window the ledger's own crossCheck uses to justify its ceiling. Every megawatt that energizes during the venture's first 12 months had its rack-power vendor chosen while the venture was still pre-launch. That leaves Year-1 demand confined to retrofit, refresh and expansion churn in the installed base — a materially smaller and slower pool than 'the market' — while the model applies its 1% to the whole SAM as if new-build spend were reachable on the venture's clock.

mechanism.text: New-build electrical BOMs for capacity energizing in the venture's Year 1 were specified at design freeze, before the venture existed as a qualified vendor; the ledger's own crossCheck concedes gear is 'locked 12–24mo before fit-out' and current long-lead conditions push lock-in earlier still

The market's growth is concentrated in exactly this pre-committed new-build pipeline (the substance of the 9% CAGR), so the contestable Year-1 pool is the installed base's refresh cycle — and what standalone PDU procurement evidence exists shows small, sometimes sole-sourced lots with 1–2 bidders, not open bake-offs

Generic obtainable-share benchmarks (1% Year 1) assume the full SAM is time-reachable; no DC-hardware-specific benchmark exists that discounts for procurement lock-in, so the model has no mechanism to encode it
obtainableFactor applied to the full SAM double-counts pre-committed new-build spend as reachable; the truly contestable Year-1 share sits at or below the band floor

risks.whyMissable: The founder's plan treats the CAGR as an accelerant and 1% as conservative precisely because the market is growing — 'we only need a tiny slice of a rising tide.' The trap is that the rising portion of the tide is the least reachable portion on a 12-month clock; the model has no node that separates committed-pipeline demand from contestable demand, so nothing in the ledger flags it.
risks.falsifier: Documented cases of CE new-build data centers running competitive rack-PDU selection within 6 months of fit-out (i.e., inside the venture's selling window), or an operator confirming PDUs are procured late as a commodity line decoupled from the electrical design freeze.

likelihood.rationale: Early electrical lock-in for new builds is well-documented industry behavior and the ledger's own crossCheck relies on a 12–24-month version of it; the offsetting uncertainty is how much PDU spend is genuinely late-cycle commodity purchasing versus BOM-committed — unquantified in every retrieved source.

perturbation.note: Year-1 capture limited to installed-base retrofit/refresh; new-build pipeline pre-committed before launch

indicators.threshold: PDU line bundled into general-electrical packages in ≥3 of 4 sampled tenders

mitigations.action: Target the installed-base refresh cycle explicitly: build the launch offer around drop-in replacement and retrofit economics (metering upgrades, DCIM integration) rather than new-build spec wins

risks.title: MID/Eichrecht-grade metering gates the billing use case in Germany — half the geography, the core intelligent-PDU value prop
risks.narrative: The intelligent PDU's premium over dumb power strips rests substantially on outlet- and rack-level metering. But in Germany — 50% of the modeled geography — any meter whose readings are used to bill sub-tenants per measured kWh falls under legal-metrology obligations (MessEG/MID): the metering chain must be conformity-assessed, not merely accurate. A pre-launch entrant whose PDU metering is 'billing-grade' in spec but not MID-certified in law cannot sell the billing use case to German colos at all, and MID module certification is its own multi-quarter, notified-body process that is nowhere on the launch-critical path. The serviceableFactor's 0.55 bundles 'EU cert stack' into one qualitative haircut and never distinguishes this instrument — yet it forecloses the highest-value slice of demand in the largest country cell.

mechanism.text: German legal metrology (MessEG, implementing MID 2014/32/EU) requires conformity-assessed meters wherever measured kWh is billed to third parties — a distinct instrument absent from the ledger's named cert set, which the digest already shows is narrower than the real EU entry stack

Colocation operators re-billing power to tenants are precisely the buyers who pay the intelligent-PDU premium for metering; Germany is 0.50 of the geography split and colocation plus hyperscale wholesale re-billing spans the majority of the segment mix

MID module B+D certification via a notified body is a multi-quarter engineering-plus-audit effort; for a pre-launch venture it cannot complete inside the same Year 1 the funnel is harvesting, so the German billing-metering demand is foreclosed for the period, not merely delayed
serviceableFactor 0.55 silently includes billing-metering demand it cannot legally reach in DE during Year 1; the reachable share sits at the band floor or below until MID certification lands

risks.whyMissable: The product meets or beats billing-class accuracy on its datasheet, so 'metering compliance' looks like a solved engineering problem. Legal metrology is a conformity regime, not an accuracy bar — a distinction invisible until a German colo's procurement lawyer asks for the MID declaration, by which point the tender is lost. And because much colo billing today is capacity-tier rather than per-kWh, early conversations can pass without the gate ever surfacing.
risks.falsifier: Confirmation from two German colocation operators (or the responsible eichamt/PTB guidance) that PDU-level metering used for tenant re-billing is exempt from MessEG conformity requirements in their billing model, or that estimate/capacity-tier billing makes MID-certified PDU metering commercially irrelevant to their purchase decision.

likelihood.rationale: The legal obligation for billed per-kWh metering is clear, but its bite depends on how much CE colo billing is actually measured-kWh at PDU level versus capacity-tier or upstream branch-circuit metering — heterogeneity the corpus documents but does not quantify.

perturbation.note: German billing-metering demand foreclosed until MID conformity; reachable share drops to band floor

indicators.signal: Billing basis in German colo contract templates and tenders (measured per-kWh at rack vs fixed-kW capacity tiers)
indicators.where: German colo operators' published service schedules; DE colo lots on TED and operator supplier portals
indicators.threshold: Per-kWh sub-metered billing specified in ≥30% of sampled DE colo offerings

indicators.where: PTB publications and Länder eichamt enforcement notices; VfEB (Eichrecht compliance association) docket
indicators.threshold: Any published guidance or enforcement action explicitly covering DC tenant re-billing meters

mitigations.action: Obtain a legal-metrology opinion plus two German colo billing-practice interviews to determine what fraction of DE PDU demand actually requires MID-conformant metering
mitigations.note: Converts the bundled 0.55 haircut into an instrument-by-instrument reach model for the largest country cell

mitigations.note: Decouples first revenue from the slowest conformity gate
